note: Sourced parameter database for Mars Habitat Sim. Every value carries its citation.

eclss.title: Environmental Control & Life Support (ECLSS)
eclss.modeling_notes: Primary source is NASA BVAD NASA/TP-2015-218570 Rev2 (Feb 2022, NTRS 20210024855) for all human-interface values; hardware from ISS-heritage ICES papers and NASA OCHMO/NASA-STD-3001. Metabolic O2/CO2/heat scale ~+/-25% (5th-percentile female to 95th-percentile male) and shift with activity via RQ (0.86 rest -> 0.95-0.96 exercise); model crew load as activity-block-weighted, not a single scalar. O2 and CO2 are coupled through RQ; water inflows (potable 3.22 + food 0.76 + metabolic 0.49) balance outflows (urine 1.42 + resp/persp 2.95 + fecal 0.10). Closure economics: OGA electrolysis + Sabatier recovers only ~48% of metabolic O2 (H2-limited); full O2-loop closure needs CH4 pyrolysis (not flight-operational). WRS reaches 98% water closure with the 2023 Brine Processor Assembly (93-94% before). For a Mars no-resupply mission the binding trade is reliability/spares/crew-time (ISS OGA/CDRA/UPA all have recurring failure modes), not steady-state efficiency; UPA recovery in particular de-rates 85%->70% from urine chemistry. Mars ISRU can supply O2 and buffer gas locally, changing whether to close loops or resupply. Fidelity tiers: L0 constant per-CM scalars for mass-balance sizing; L1 activity-block daily model with atmosphere ODEs and hardware capacity as f(ppCO2, production rate); L2 sub-sol physics with adsorption isotherms (CDRA), electrolysis polarization curves (OGA), Sabatier H2-limited kinetics, condensing-HX latent/sensible split, and a nodal water network with de-ratable recovery efficiencies. Cross-couples to thermal (sensible/latent heat), power (OGA/CDRA/Sabatier draw), and logistics/ISRU (O2/N2/water/spares).

parameters.name: Crew O2 consumption per crew-member-day
parameters.source: NASA BVAD NASA/TP-2015-218570 Rev2 (2022), Table 4-1 nominal; Table 3-31 gives 0.895
parameters.notes: 82 kg reference CM, exercising to hold mass. Range = lower/upper design bounds (~5th-percentile female to 95th-percentile male, ~+/-25%). Legacy value 0.816 kg/CM-d.

parameters.name: Crew CO2 production per crew-member-day
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Rev2 Table 4-1 (nominal 1.08); Table 3-31 CO2 load 1.085; RQ 0.86
parameters.notes: 1.04 = O2*RQ*(44/32) consistent point estimate; BVAD Table 4-1 nominal is 1.08. Carry with O2 as a coupled pair.

parameters.name: Respiratory quotient (mol CO2 / mol O2)
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Rev2 Table 3-31 (Ewert 2021 MetMan)
parameters.notes: 0.86 rest/sleep, 0.95 aerobic exercise, 0.96 resistive exercise.

parameters.name: Crew total metabolic heat load
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Rev2 Table 3-31
parameters.notes: = 143.8 W average per CM. Sensible 6.308 MJ/CM-d (73.0 W), latent 6.118 MJ/CM-d (70.8 W). Sensible->cabin air, latent->condensing HX.

parameters.name: Crew total human water consumption (drink + food prep)
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Rev2 Table 4-20 (drinking 2.00 + food rehydration 0.50)
parameters.notes: Table 3-31 potable water content 3.217 kg/CM-d includes 0.717 kg exercise-perspiration allowance. Drinking alone 2.0 kg/CM-d.

parameters.name: Crew hygiene water use (mission-class dependent)
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Rev2 Table 4-20
parameters.notes: ISS/transit minimal hygiene 0.7 (urinal flush 0.3 + personal hygiene 0.4). Early planetary base 1.98; mature base 6.62 (adds hand wash, shower, laundry, dish wash). verified correction: BVAD Table 4-20 lists hand wash as TBD/n-a for planetary bases; the 4.08 kg/CM-d hand-wash figure is a WASTEWATER stream (Table 4-21), not a Table 4-20 usage total. Do not add it to hygiene-usage totals.

parameters.name: Crew total water use (excl. payload)
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Rev2 Table 4-20
parameters.notes: Transit vehicle 3.2; early planetary base 4.48; mature base 9.12; ISS 5.38 incl 2.18 payload.

parameters.name: Crew urine wastewater output (urine + flush)
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Rev2 Table 4-21 (ISS: urine 1.20 + flush 0.30)
parameters.notes: Table 3-31 urine water 1.420 kg/CM-d. Transit/base flush raises total to 1.8-2.0 kg/CM-d.

parameters.name: Crew latent humidity condensate output
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Rev2 Table 4-21
parameters.notes: Latent load removed by condensing heat exchanger, feeds WPA. Table 3-31 respiration+perspiration water is 2.946 kg/CM-d.

parameters.name: Cabin total pressure (setpoint options)
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Rev2 Table 4-1; NASA exploration atmosphere studies
parameters.notes: 101.3 kPa sea-level (ISS); 70.3 kPa (10.2 psia) and 56.5 kPa (8.2 psia, 34% O2) reduced-pressure exploration options for shorter EVA prebreathe.

parameters.name: Cabin oxygen partial pressure (nominal setpoint)
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Rev2 Table 4-1; Norcross 2013; NASA-STD-3001
parameters.notes: Nominal ~21.2 kPa (Earth normal). Measurable impairment until acclimatized 17.2-18.6; min allowable <=1 h is 15.2-17.2 kPa. Upper bounded by flammability at higher O2 fraction.

parameters.name: Cabin nitrogen partial pressure (101.3 kPa atmosphere)
parameters.source: Derived: total 101.3 - ppO2 21.2 - ppCO2 ~0.4 - pH2O ~1.6
parameters.notes: Buffer gas ~78 mol%. Non-consumed except leakage and airlock/EVA losses; makeup dominated by airlock cycling, not metabolism.

parameters.name: Cabin CO2 partial pressure limit (NASA-STD-3001, 1-h avg)
parameters.source: NASA-STD-3001 Vol 2 Rev D [V2 6004]; NASA OCHMO-TB-004
parameters.notes: 3 mmHg = 0.40 kPa 1-h average limit (headache <1.4%). 7-day <2.5 mmHg (0.33 kPa) for <1% headache. ISS observed band 1-9 mmHg (0.13-1.2 kPa). BVAD design nominal 0.507 kPa. Formal CO2 SMAC deleted.

parameters.name: Cabin temperature setpoint
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Rev2 Table 4-1
parameters.notes: ~23 C nominal, 18-27 C range. Relative humidity nominal 40% (range 25-75%).

parameters.name: Habitat atmosphere leakage rate (per module, nominal design)
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Rev2 Table 4-1, Sec 4.1
parameters.notes: Nominal = 2x the ~0.01 kg/day observed in module test (Gateway-derived margin). As fraction: 0.01-0.14 %/day. Whole-ISS observed leakage ~0.195 kg/day (ESA). verified correction: whole-ISS 'observed 0.195 kg/day (ESA)' is unverified; NASA trending gives ~0.18-0.23 kg/day (2008-2011). Post-2019 Zvezda structural-crack leakage is far higher (~0.45-1.7 kg/day) and not representative of design leakage. Design value 0.02 kg/day/module is unaffected.

parameters.name: OGA maximum O2 production rate (PEM electrolysis)
parameters.source: ICES-2023-311, Status of the Advanced OGA (NTRS 20230008013)
parameters.notes: 20.4 lb/day at 46.9 A max stack current; supports 10.88 CM at 0.85 kg O2/CM-d. Selectable rate; standby 1.0 A. Cumulative 2007-2023: 11,903 kg O2, 1,488 kg H2.

parameters.name: OGA electrolysis specific energy (cell stack)
parameters.source: Derived from ICES-2023-311 (46.9 A, ~1.7 V/cell, ~28 cells => 2.23 kW at 9.25 kg/day)
parameters.notes: Stack level 5.8 kWh/kg (thermoneutral min 4.97 at 1.48 V/cell). System level ~7-12 kWh/kg incl balance-of-plant and part-load; flight estimates ~2.4 kW for ~6-crew O2.

parameters.name: CDRA (4BMS) CO2 removal capacity
parameters.unit: kg/day CO2 per CDRA (20.4 SCFM nominal flow, 2.0 torr inlet ppCO2)
parameters.source: Knox & Stanley, ICES-2015-165, NTRS 20150016500, Sec. II & Table 3 (PW 3.17 kg/day @461 W; PF 3.49 kg/day @486 W at 20 SCFM / 2 torr)
parameters.notes: 4.1 CM-eq at 2.0 torr; 10.5 CM-eq at 5.0 torr; roughly linear in inlet ppCO2. Nominal air flow 34.7 m3/h (20.4 SCFM). ISS 6-CM CO2 throughput ~6.3 kg/day. Per-unit design 6-7 CM. verified correction: the 4.1/10.5 CM-eq capacities were demonstrated in a HIGH-FLOW 25 SCFM, 90-min half-cycle config (+200 W heater); nominal-flow (20.4 SCFM) measured removal is only 3.17-3.49 kg CO2/day (~3.0-3.4 CM-eq), so the prior capacity curve overstated nominal CDRA capacity by ~20-25%. Use 4.1/10.5 CM-eq ONLY for the high-flow configuration.

parameters.name: CDRA nominal power (order of magnitude)
parameters.source: Estimate: heaters + blower + valves; +200 W heater at high-flow config (NTRS 20150016500)
parameters.notes: Open literature gives flow/capacity cleanly but not a single clean nominal power. Thermal-swing heater power dominates and is duty-cycle dependent.

parameters.name: Sabatier CO2-reduction O2-loop closure (metabolic O2 recovered)
parameters.source: NTRS 20100033195 Sabatier CRA; NASA SCOR
parameters.notes: H2-limited: OGA yields 2 H2 per O2 but Sabatier needs 4 H2 per CO2; CH4 vented. ISS estimated ~47%. Water output ~907 kg/yr (7-CM sizing). >50% needs CH4 pyrolysis/Bosch (not operational).

parameters.name: Water Recovery System overall recovery fraction (UPA+WPA+BPA)
parameters.source: NASA water recovery milestone, 20 Jun 2023
parameters.notes: 98% demonstrated with Brine Processor Assembly (2023); 93-94% pre-BPA baseline. Use 98% best-case closed loop, 93% conservative.

parameters.name: UPA urine water recovery fraction (de-ratable)
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Rev2 Sec 4.3.3 (Carter); Wikipedia ISS ECLSS
parameters.notes: US segment design 85%; de-rated to ~70% on orbit due to calcium-sulfate precipitation in high-Ca urine. Russian ~70%. UPA capacity 9 kg/day (6-CM). Treat as de-ratable parameter.

parameters.name: WPA expendable filter/bed resupply mass
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Rev2 Sec 4.3.3 (Carrasquillo 2005)
parameters.notes: Multifiltration beds + ion-exchange bed. Drives logistics for closed-loop water on no-resupply missions.

parameters.name: Total ISS ECLSS average electrical power
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Rev2 Table 3-2
parameters.notes: Whole ECLSS average for ISS-class crew. Thermal Control System separately 8.72 kW. OGA is the largest single ECLSS draw.

parameters.name: Regenerative ECLSS hardware mass per crew (ESM sizing estimate)
parameters.source: Estimate: engineering ESM order-of-magnitude incl racks + spares
parameters.notes: Individual ISS ORU masses are not cleanly partitioned in open literature; AOGA cell-stack module alone ~136 kg. Use as placeholder for system-level trades; refine per architecture. verified correction: the ~136 kg (300 lb) launch-config mass is the LEGACY ISS OGA Hydrogen ORU (per ICES-2023-311), not a next-gen AOGA module; the AOGA redesign is intended to reduce it. Do not use 136 kg as a next-gen mass assumption.

starship.title: Starship Launch & Transport Vehicle
starship.modeling_notes: Use company claims as optimistic bounds, not expected values: as of 2026-07 Starship V3 has flown once (Flight 12, 2026-05-22, partial success) and ship-to-ship propellant transfer, Mars EDL, and surface relaunch are all undemonstrated. Ship dry mass is the master uncertainty (100-210 t across sources) and should be the correlated driver in Monte-Carlo runs: it simultaneously sets Mars-landed payload (rocket-equation EDL closure with dv_land ~500-800 m/s after aerodynamic deceleration from 7.5 km/s entry), tanker count (N = m_TMI/m_deliver, m_deliver 100-200 t), and Earth-return feasibility (dv 6.4-7.5 km/s from surface to TEI; propellant = (m_dry+m_pl)(exp(dv/g0/Isp)-1), Isp ~375-380 s). Return propellant baseline 1,200 t/ship (21.7% CH4 at O/F 3.6) implies ~600 t water and ~600 kW-class continuous ISRU power per ship per synodic period (~780 sols). Boiloff: model Q = k_e*A*dT/L with Mars diurnal ambient; passive insulated ~0.25 %/sol (0.05-2), zero-boiloff costs ~40 kW_e (~990 kWh/sol). Cargo offloading is a first-class constraint: ~33.5 m lowering height, <=10 t crane budget, tipping limits - budget equipment mass and sols per ship. Fidelity tiers: L0 scalar averages above; L1 analytic per-window (rocket equation + opportunity-dependent dv table + linear boiloff); L2 sub-sol 3-DOF EDL Monte-Carlo, 2-node tank thermal with diurnal forcing, and full ascent integration. Apply a schedule/performance haircut to all SpaceX figures (historically ~2 windows late, 30-50% payload-optimistic).

parameters.name: Starship V3 full stack height
parameters.source: SpaceX claim (Musk presentation 2025-05-29); Flight 12 vehicle (2026-05-22)
parameters.notes: V2 was 123.1 m. Booster 72.3 m + ship ~52.1 m.

parameters.name: Starship (ship/upper stage) V3 length
parameters.source: Derived: 124.4 m stack minus 72.3 m booster; one Wikipedia table lists 61 m (likely V4 conflation)
parameters.notes: 9 m diameter cylinder; payload section top ~33.5 m above ground when landed (Howard et al. 2023).

parameters.name: Starship body diameter
parameters.source: SpaceX, all orbital-class Starships

parameters.name: Payload to LEO, fully reusable (company claim)
parameters.source: SpaceX claim (Musk, 2025-05-29 presentation: '100+ t'; SpaceX materials up to 150 t)
parameters.notes: Not yet demonstrated: V2 flew ~35 t of simulators; V3 Flight 12 (2026-05-22) deployed 20 Starlink simulators + 2 satellites.

parameters.name: Payload to LEO, fully expendable (company claim)
parameters.source: SpaceX/Musk claims 2024-2025 (company claim)
parameters.notes: Never planned for Mars ops; useful as an upper performance bound.

parameters.name: Payload to Mars surface (SpaceX claim)
parameters.source: SpaceX claim (2017-2026, incl. Musk 2025-05-29 presentation): 100 t class to Mars surface (corroborated by Heldmann et al. 2022)
parameters.notes: Company claim, not demonstrated. Landed mass ~200+ t would be ~200x largest previous Mars landing. verified correction: the '$100M/t Mars cargo pricing NET 2028' detail is NOT present at the cited Aerospace America URL and has been removed from the source string; the 100 t value and URL are retained.

parameters.name: Payload to Mars surface (independent working estimate, early flights)
parameters.source: Estimate: midpoint between demonstrated V2 LEO capability (~35 t) and claim; bounded by Maiwald et al. 2024 optimistic max 114.4 t and their finding of infeasibility at realistic (210 t) dry mass
parameters.notes: Rapp 2024: ~950 t of 1200 t burned at TMI, ~30 t midcourse, ~220-250 t left for EDL. Use as Monte-Carlo variable correlated with dry mass.

parameters.name: Pressurized (crew) forward volume
parameters.source: Heldmann et al. 2022 New Space ('on order of 1,100 m3 forward space'); SpaceX historical claim 1,000 m3
parameters.notes: Crew configuration only; cargo config uses 614 m3 payload bay.

parameters.name: Cargo payload bay volume
parameters.source: Wikipedia (SpaceX Starship spacecraft page, from Users Guide-era data)
parameters.notes: Nominal deployment doors 3 m x 3 m (Heldmann et al. 2022).

parameters.name: Ship dry mass (V3 cargo variant, estimate)
parameters.source: Estimate: Musk 2021 stated ~100 t (Block 1); Maiwald et al. 2024 bottom-up crewed estimate 210.5 t incl. 20% margin; no official V3 figure
parameters.notes: Master uncertainty of the whole transport model - drives Mars payload, refill count, and return feasibility. Crewed variants heavier than cargo/tanker.

parameters.name: Ship propellant load (V3)
parameters.source: Musk 'Road to Making Life Multiplanetary' 2025-05-29 (ship: 1,550 t propellant, 52.1 m, ~1,600 tf, six engines)
parameters.notes: At O/F 3.6: ~348 t CH4 + ~1252 t LOX. ~2% unusable residuals typical assumption. verified correction: May 2025 presentation gave 1,550 t (not 1,600 t) for the flown V3 ship; 1,600 t is near-term growth kept as range max. The 1,600 t Wikipedia 'Block 3' column mixes future-stretch specs.

parameters.name: Super Heavy propellant load (V3)
parameters.source: Musk 'Road to Making Life Multiplanetary' 2025-05-29 (3,650 t; growth to ~3,700-4,000 t mentioned); Wikipedia 'SpaceX Super Heavy' Block 3 (3,650 t, 72.3 m); Tesla Oracle 2026-03-12 B19 load report (3,650 t)
parameters.notes: V2 booster was 3650 t. verified correction: 4,050 t was a future stretched-vehicle figure conflated from a Wikipedia table; the flown V3 booster loads 3,650 t (volume check refutes 4,050 t in a 72.3 m booster). V1 was 3,250-3,400 t; V2/3-era is 3,650 t. 4,050 t is future growth only.

parameters.name: Full stack gross liftoff mass (V3)
parameters.source: Derived: ~5,200 t propellant (1,550 t ship + 3,650 t booster, May 2025 figures) + ~400-450 t stage dry masses
parameters.notes: verified correction: rocketlaunch.org 5,250 t was the propellant sum mislabeled as GLOM (internally inconsistent: propellant alone exceeded it). Realistic V3 GLOM ~5,600-5,750 t; liftoff T/W at 80.8 MN is ~1.45, not 1.57. Do not use rocketlaunch.org for GLOM.

parameters.name: Raptor oxidizer-to-fuel mass ratio (LOX:CH4)
parameters.source: Wikipedia SpaceX Raptor (3.6:1); Heldmann et al. 2022 use ~3.5
parameters.notes: CH4 mass fraction = 1/(1+3.6) = 21.7% of total propellant.

parameters.name: Raptor 3 sea-level thrust (rated)
parameters.source: SpaceX claim (X post 2024-08-04: 280 tf); flown at ~250 tf nominal on early V3 flights per Wikipedia
parameters.notes: 280 tf = 2.746 MN. Engine mass 1,525 kg (+195 kg vehicle-side). Chamber pressure 330-350 bar.

parameters.name: Raptor 3 sea-level specific impulse
parameters.source: SpaceX claim (X post 2024-08-04)
parameters.notes: Company claim; not independently verified.

parameters.name: Raptor 3 vacuum (RVac) specific impulse
parameters.source: SpaceX claim/target (2024); Maiwald et al. 2024 use 378 s
parameters.notes: RVac thrust ~275 tf nominal, 306 tf target.

parameters.name: Ship engine count (V3)
parameters.source: Musk presentation 2025-05-29 ('six engines now, nine on a future version'); Starship Flight 12 flew S39 with six Raptors (splashdown on 2 of 3 center engines)
parameters.notes: Landing uses 2-3 sea-level gimbaling engines (Flight 12 splashed down on 2). verified correction: V3 ships fly 6 engines (3 sea-level + 3 RVac), not 9; the 3 SL + 6 RVac layout is the FUTURE config. Using 9 overstates Mars ascent/landing thrust by 50%. 9 kept as range max (future variant).

parameters.name: Super Heavy engine count
parameters.source: Flight-demonstrated (Flight 12, 2026-05-22: all 33 Raptor 3 lit)

parameters.name: Super Heavy liftoff thrust (V3)
parameters.source: rocketlaunch.org (80,806 kN, i.e. ~250 tf/engine); 33 x 280 tf rated = 90.6 MN; SpaceX '10,000 tf' aspiration with Raptor 3.x
parameters.notes: T/W at liftoff ~1.55-1.75 depending on rating.

parameters.name: Propellant delivered to LEO depot per tanker flight
parameters.source: Range across sources: Rapp 2024 uses 100 t; New Space Economy 2026 claims ~200 t for dedicated V3 tankers; no flight demonstration yet
parameters.notes: Ship-to-ship transfer not yet demonstrated as of 2026-07; planned on upcoming V3 flights.

parameters.name: Tanker flights to fill one Mars-bound ship in LEO
parameters.source: Rapp (Aerospace America 2026): 12 at 100 t/flight for 1200 t; SpaceX claims 8-10 (HLS-era); GAO/NASA 16-19 incl. boiloff
parameters.notes: N = (m_TMI - residuals)/m_deliver + depot boiloff losses. 5-ship 2026/27 fleet => ~60 tanker launches.

parameters.name: Delta-v, Mars surface to trans-Earth injection (direct ascent)
parameters.source: Composed from Wooster et al. 2007-heritage values quoted by Rapp/IgMin: surface->500 km LMO 4.3 km/s, surface->1-sol orbit 5.6 km/s (incl. losses), + TEI 1.3-2.0 km/s
parameters.notes: Opportunity-dependent. Full V3 tanks give ~9.3 km/s at 145 t burnout - margin exists if dry mass <=160 t.

parameters.name: CH4+LOX to relaunch one ship from Mars to Earth-return
parameters.source: Heldmann et al. 2022: full V1-class refill 1200 t = 933 t LOX + 267 t CH4; Rapp 2024: ~920 t for stripped ship; rocket-equation minimum ~780 t (125 t dry + 20 t payload, 6.9 km/s, Isp 375 s)
parameters.notes: Requires ~600 t water + Sabatier/electrolysis ISRU per ship per synodic period (~780 sols); ~600 kW continuous for the LOX share alone (Hecht, quoted in Aerospace America 2026). Maiwald et al. 2024 find return infeasible at 210 t dry mass.

parameters.name: Passive cryo boiloff rate on Mars surface (insulated, fraction of full load)
parameters.source: Estimate: scaled from Liu 2017 (NTRS 20170011674) Mars worst-case heat flux 15.4-18.1 W/m2 through 22 mm LCI (k=2 mW/m-K) over ~900 m2 tank area => 3.6-6.5 t/day on LOX (h_fg 213 kJ/kg); Handmer 2025: 20-layer MLI cuts heat ~99%, <1 t/day
parameters.notes: Bare stainless tanks: several %/sol. Well-insulated space reference: 0.016 %/day LOX (NTRS 20180004710). No SpaceX figure published.

parameters.name: Electrical power for zero-boiloff storage of a full ship on Mars
parameters.source: Estimate: scaled from Liu 2017 (8.3-11.5 kW_e reverse turbo-Brayton per 3.5-3.7 kW heat lift at ~162-186 m2 insulated tank area, cryocooler eff. 70% of Carnot) to ~900 m2 Starship tank area
parameters.notes: ~990 kWh per sol at 40 kW. Radiator area ~15 m2 per 11.5 kW rejected; 7 mbar CO2 convection nearly useless, radiation-dominated.

parameters.name: Mars landing accuracy (CEP, early flights)
parameters.source: Estimate: Earth tower catches (2024-2025) show dm-class precision with GNSS; Mars requires terrain-relative navigation (Perseverance TRN ~5 m map-relative, 2021); powered descent + TRN plausibly 10-100 m CEP, first landings up to km-class
parameters.notes: Gerstenmaier 'half a centimeter' Flight 4 splashdown claim (2024) widely judged a misstatement. With landed beacons/pads: ~10 m.

parameters.name: Cargo lowering height from payload section (landed ship)
parameters.source: Howard et al., AIAA ASCEND 2023 (NTRS 20220010430): lowering a 90-t habitat 'from the upper section of Starship (~33.5 m high)'
parameters.notes: Crane budget <=10 t (JSC study; ~5.3 t beams alone); tipping prevention needed (nosecone counterweight concepts); doors 3x3 m nominal. SpaceX has published no offloading solution.

parameters.name: Earth-Mars transit time (crew/cargo, company claim)
parameters.source: SpaceX claim (Musk 2025-05-29: 80-150 days, avg ~115); Ataee & Elvander Sci Rep 2025 find 90-104 d feasible with 15 tankers/crewed ship
parameters.notes: ~112 sols at 115 days. Slower ~180-210 d Hohmann-like transfers reduce TMI propellant and entry velocity (7.5 km/s entry cited for nominal).

parameters.name: Mars-bound ships in first uncrewed window (2026/27, plan)
parameters.source: SpaceX claim (Musk 2025-05-29 presentation: 5 uncrewed V3 ships carrying Optimus robots, 50% probability of making window; ramp 20 -> 100 -> 500 in later windows)
parameters.notes: Depends on orbital refilling demonstration in 2026. Musk 2026-03 X post reaffirmed end-2026 departure intent.

power_solar.title: Solar Power & Energy Storage
power_solar.modeling_notes: L0 (scalar): E_sol[kWh/sol] = P_installed[kW] x EPH x DF x eta_sys, with EPH ~5.5 kWh/sol per installed kW (equator, climatological dust, kW rated at eta*A*590 W/m2), DF~0.85 maintained arrays, eta_sys~0.90 PMAD; storm risk = output x0.3 for ~100 sols with p~0.3 per Mars year. L1 (daily/seasonal): Gob(Ls)=590[1+e cos(Ls-251)]^2/(1-e^2)^2; declination sin(d)=sin(25.19)sin(Ls); daily TOA insolation Hobh=(24/pi)Gob[(pi*wss/180)sin(phi)sin(d)+cos(phi)cos(d)sin(wss)] with wss=acos(-tan phi tan d); surface H = Hobh x T(tau) with T = {0.86@0.4, 0.71@1, 0.41@3, 0.26@5, 0.20@6}; tau(Ls) piecewise 0.45 clear/0.9 dusty seasons + stochastic regional storms + GDS process (Bernoulli 0.3/MY, onset Ls 185-310, peak tau N(9,1), exp decay 43 sols); dust factor DF ODE: -0.28%/sol deposition, Poisson cleaning (site param 0-6/MY, restore to 0.9) or scheduled active cleaning. L2 (sub-sol): cos z = sin phi sin d + cos phi cos d cos w (w=15T-180); beam Gbh=Gob cos z exp(-tau/cos z); global Gh=Gob cos z f(z,tau,al)/(1-al) with bilinear interpolation of the TM-103623 f-table (albedo 0.1; extrapolate log-linearly for tau>6); diffuse=Gh-Gbh; tilted plane via beam AOI + isotropic diffuse; cell temp from energy balance (alpha=0.88, eps=0.85, thin CO2 convection) with eta(T)=eta_ref[1-0.0022(T-301K)] and spectral factor ~0.95; battery SOC integration (round-trip 0.92, DOD 0.7). Validation targets: VL1/VL2 daily insolation tables (TM-103623 Table V), InSight 4.6->0.5 kWh/sol power history, MSL 97% UV drop at MY34 GDS peak. Key trap: storm output scales with the net-flux function (factor ~3 loss at tau 5), NOT exp(-tau) (factor ~150); diffuse light keeps solar viable through storms at reduced power.

parameters.name: Mars mean solar irradiance at top of atmosphere (TOA, normal incidence)
parameters.source: Computed from TSI 1361 W/m2 at 1.5236915 AU; Appelbaum & Flood NASA TM-102299 use 590 (S=1371). Range = aphelion..perihelion.
parameters.notes: Use 590 W/m2 when implementing the A&F lookup tables for self-consistency; difference is 0.7%.

parameters.name: TOA beam irradiance at perihelion (Ls~251 deg)
parameters.notes: Perihelion coincides with southern summer and the dust storm season.

parameters.name: TOA beam irradiance at aphelion (Ls~71 deg)
parameters.source: NASA TM-102299 eq. (4)
parameters.notes: Peri/aphelion flux ratio 1.45 - dominant deterministic seasonal driver.

parameters.name: Mars orbital eccentricity
parameters.source: NASA TM-102299 nomenclature (e = 0.093377); NSSDC Mars Fact Sheet 0.0935

parameters.name: Areocentric longitude of perihelion
parameters.source: Modern ephemeris/NSSDC Mars Fact Sheet (251 deg); A&F TM-102299 used 248 deg
parameters.notes: Use 251 in new code; <1% insolation effect vs 248.

parameters.name: Mars obliquity (axial tilt) for solar declination
parameters.source: NSSDC Mars Fact Sheet; A&F TM-102299 eq. (7) used 24.936 deg. sin(dec) = sin(obliquity) sin(Ls)

parameters.name: Visible dust optical depth, clear (aphelion) season, low latitudes
parameters.source: MER 5-Mars-year record (Lemmon et al. 2015, NTRS 20150008268): Spirit <0.3, Opportunity <0.5 in aphelion season; Viking 'typically about 0.5' (TM-102299); ICES-2019-181 (NTRS 20190027303) non-storm background tau ~0.4
parameters.notes: 880 nm solar-imaging standard; minima ~0.2 observed. verified correction: prior source string misquoted ICES-2019-181 as 'planetary background ~0.48'; that paper states ~0.4. Value 0.45 (range 0.3-0.6) is unchanged and remains defensible.

parameters.name: Dust optical depth, dusty (perihelion) season background (no global storm)
parameters.source: MER/MSL/Viking records: regional storms to tau 1.2-3.6 superposed (Lemmon et al. 2015; TM-102299 VL1 record max 3.25-3.6 in 1977 storms)
parameters.notes: Ls 180-360; regional storms (days-weeks) add +1 to +2.5.

parameters.name: Peak dust optical depth during a global (planet-encircling) dust storm
parameters.source: 2018/MY34 GDS: MSL Mastcam tau=8.5 (Guzewich et al. 2019 GRL); Opportunity tau>10.7 lower limit (NTRS 20190027303)
parameters.notes: Site-dependent; A&F f-tables end at tau=6, extrapolate beyond.

parameters.name: Global dust storm occurrence rate
parameters.source: 3 GDS in MY25/28/34 over ~10 Mars years (NTRS 20190027303); historical ~1 per 3 Mars years, aperiodic with gaps up to 6 MY
parameters.notes: All observed GDS initiate at Ls 185-310 deg; model as Bernoulli per Mars year.

parameters.name: Global dust storm optical-depth exponential decay time
parameters.source: MSL MY34 record: exponential decay, e-folding 43 sols (NTRS 20190027303, citing Guzewich et al. 2019)
parameters.notes: Rise time ~1-2 weeks; total enhanced-tau duration ~100 sols.

parameters.name: Design-case dust storm duration at tau>=5 (NASA HEOMD sizing case)
parameters.source: Rucker et al., Solar vs. Fission Surface Power for Mars (NTRS 20160002628): 120-day storm at average optical depth 5
parameters.notes: Conservative envelope vs observed ~100 sols enhanced tau in 2018.

parameters.name: Solar array dust deposition (obscuration) rate, horizontal array, between cleanings
parameters.source: Pathfinder 0.28%/sol first 30 sols (NASA/TM-2004-213367); InSight 0.2%/sol observed, 0.28%/sol steady-state model (Lorenz et al. 2020, doi:10.1029/2019EA000992); MER long-term ~0.14%/sol; envelope 0.05-2%/sol (Lorenz et al. 2021 P&SS 105337)
parameters.notes: x3-10 during storm fallout; ~x0.1 for near-vertical panels (Landis used 0.028%/sol).

parameters.name: Dust factor restored by a major natural cleaning event
parameters.source: MER flight history: 2014 Opportunity events boosted power ~70%, arrays imaged near-clean; frequency is site-specific - InSight saw no significant cleaning in 4 years (Lorenz et al. 2021)
parameters.notes: Model as Poisson 0-6 events/Mars-year (site wind climate); do not rely on for design - assume active cleaning restores 0.95-1.0.

parameters.name: Flight III-V triple-junction cell efficiency, BOL, AM0 28 degC
parameters.source: Spectrolab XTJ Prime datasheet 7/2016 (company claim, flight product): 30.7% production average; SolAero ZTJ 29.5%; MER-era 3J ~27.5%; Mars-spectrum-adapted ~32% (Landis NTRS 20070010752)
parameters.notes: Radiation degradation negligible under Mars atmosphere (~20 g/cm2 shielding).

parameters.name: Relative maximum-power temperature coefficient, 3J cell
parameters.source: Computed from Spectrolab XTJ Prime datasheet coefficients (dVmp/dT=-6.5 mV/degC on 2.390 V; dJmp/dT=+8.9 uA/cm2/degC on 17.4 mA/cm2)
parameters.notes: Mars arrays run cold (-50 to +10 degC daytime): +8 to +15% relative efficiency vs 28 degC rating.

parameters.name: Efficiency multiplier for red-shifted dusty-sky spectrum (3J cells, tau~1)
parameters.source: Landis & Hyatt, The Solar Spectrum on the Martian Surface and its Effect on Photovoltaic Performance (NTRS 20070010752): dust absorbs blue, top junction becomes current-limiting
parameters.notes: Worsens with tau; ~1.0 for Si or spectrum-matched cells.

parameters.name: Mars surface array areal mass, system level (blanket + structure + deployment + electrical)
parameters.source: Compact Telescoping Surface Array study (NTRS 20190000437): 1500 kg per 1000 m2 goal, blanket 0.5-1.0 kg/m2; Rucker 2016 UltraFlex-based point ~2.9 kg/m2
parameters.notes: CTSA is a TRL 4-5 design goal; UltraFlex value has flight-adjacent pedigree.

parameters.name: Array specific power at Mars surface noon, clear sky, equator
parameters.source: CTSA study (NTRS 20190000437): 50-80 kW daytime from ~1500 kg, 1000 m2 (200 W/kg at 1 AU equivalent); UltraFlex flight wings 100-150 W/kg at 1 AU
parameters.notes: Equivalent ~19-30 kg per noon-peak kW, generation hardware only (no storage).

parameters.name: Complete solar power system mass per kW continuous (day+night) load
parameters.source: Rucker AIAA Space 2016 (NTRS 20160011275): 11,713 kg Jezero system for ~35 kW day / 26.8 kW night (12,679 kg Columbus); storm-hardened variant 9,800 kg for 22 kW keep-alive (NTRS 20160002628). Fission comparison: 4x10 kWe Kilopower = 9,154 kg (~230 kg/kW)
parameters.notes: Includes generation + Li-ion storage + structure + thermal, excludes lander-to-lander PMAD; equatorial site, modest storm requirement.

parameters.name: Li-ion battery specific energy, pack/system level, Mars-surface rated
parameters.source: JPL D-101146 Energy Storage Technologies report (2017): flight state-of-practice packs <100 Wh/kg (Yardney MER/Phoenix/MSL heritage); development targets 150-250 Wh/kg; modern cells 240-270 Wh/kg imply near-term packs 150-180
parameters.notes: Settlement-era value is a projection; flight-qualified today is the low end.

parameters.name: Li-ion battery round-trip energy efficiency (system level, incl. converters)
parameters.source: Estimate: Li-ion cell-level 94-97% from terrestrial literature; Tesla Powerwall 92.5% DC round-trip (company claim); system studies 84-96%
parameters.notes: Degrades at low temperature and high C-rate.

parameters.name: Allowable depth of discharge for multi-Mars-year cycle life
parameters.source: Estimate: Li-ion cycle-life curves for >2000 cycles (3+ Mars years of daily cycling); JPL D-101146 mission requirements cite 100% DOD only for short missions
parameters.notes: Sizing: battery kWh = night load x 12.3 h / (DOD x round-trip eff).

parameters.name: Regenerative H2/O2 fuel cell specific energy for multi-sol (storm) storage
parameters.source: JPL D-101146 human-lander target >500 Wh/kg; CTSA study baselines 10 kW RFC night power; no flight heritage
parameters.notes: Round-trip efficiency only ~40-55%; beats Li-ion on mass for >30-50 h of storage.

parameters.name: Daily global insolation, horizontal surface, equator, clear sky (tau 0.4)
parameters.source: Integrated from A&F TM-103623 f-table (this work, 4.05 at equinox); matches TM-103623 Table V VL1 max 4.18 kWh/m2-sol at Ls 140
parameters.notes: Range covers aphelion/perihelion Gob swing at low latitude.

parameters.name: Daily global insolation, horizontal, equator, global-storm design tau=5
parameters.source: Integrated from A&F TM-103623 f-table (this work); consistent with Lemmon et al. 2015 flight observation that tau>4 cuts surface insolation only by factor 2-3 due to forward scattering
parameters.notes: At tau 8.5 (GDS peak) extrapolated ~0.55-0.7 kWh/m2/sol; NOT e^-tau scaling.

parameters.name: Worst-sol daily insolation at 48 deg N (winter + Viking-year dust storm)
parameters.source: NASA TM-103623 Table V, VL2 (47.7 N) minimum 315 Wh/m2-day at Ls 280; VL1 (22.3 N) minimum 935 Wh/m2-day for comparison
parameters.notes: Latitude penalty: worst-sol/best-sol = 7.5% at 48N vs 22% at 22N. NASA crewed solar studies restrict to |lat|<~30 deg.

parameters.name: Equivalent peak-sun hours per sol (daily energy / noon power), equator, horizontal
parameters.source: Computed from A&F model (this work): 7.44 h at tau 0.4, falling to 6.4 h at tau 5-6 (profile flattens as diffuse dominates)
parameters.notes: Distillation: kWh/sol per installed kW (rated at 590 W/m2 reference) = 6.9 (tau 0.4), 5.7 (tau 1), 3.3 (tau 3), 2.0 (tau 5); multiply by dust factor and PMAD efficiency.

power_nuclear.title: Nuclear Power (Fission & RTG)
power_nuclear.modeling_notes: Fission is the constant term in a Mars power model: output is independent of latitude, season, sol cycle, and dust optical depth, which is precisely why NASA's 2024 Architecture Concept Review baselined it for crewed Mars. L0: model each unit as constant net power P_rated with mass M[kg]=562*P^0.673 (HP-Stirling, 10-100 kWe, incl 20% MGA; GC-Brayton 863*P^0.580; use 1750-2000 kg for a crew-shielded 10 kWe Kilopower), energy per sol = P*24.6597 h (40 kWe -> 986 kWh/sol, ~0.145 kWh/sol per landed kg). L1: add (a) discrete k-of-n string reliability - 4x25% strings, >=75% power after any single fault, restart <=8 h autonomous; (b) slow drift 0-1%/yr (dust on radiators, converter aging - reactor itself is flat because the constant-temperature core load-follows passively and rod motion nulls burnup droop); (c) siting trade: shadow shield {0.5 km: 2700 kg, 1 km: 1370 kg, 2 km: 700 kg} + cable ~45-100 kg/km at 2.8-3 kV vs buried option (shield mass mostly deleted, keep-out 1 km -> 100 m, but requires excavation equipment); (d) reproduce Rucker solar-vs-fission: solar sized for 22 kWe keep-alive through 120-sol tau=5 storm = 9.8-12.7 t vs fission 7-9.2 t at 35-50 kWe. L2: point-kinetics with strong negative temperature feedback (KRUSTY: <4% core-temp deviation for >175% load swings, +14 degC on total loss of cooling), Stirling eta ~ 0.5*Carnot(1050 K/420 K) ~ 26% converter, PMAD 83-90%, end-to-end ~78-83%; radiator Q = A*[eps*sigma*(Tr^4-Tsky^4)*Fsky + eps*sigma*(Tr^4-Tgnd^4)*Fgnd + h*(Tr-Tatm)] - alpha*A*G_solar with eps=0.85 EOL, alpha=0.2, Tr=403-433 K, Mars Tsky~150-170 K, h~1-5 W/m2K in CO2 (lunar-sized 3.5-4 m2/kWe is conservative on Mars); dose field D(r)=D_1km*(1000/r)^2 with 5 rem/yr inside 36-deg shielded cone at 1 km, ~50 rem/yr outside cone, months-long cooldown clock before crew approach after shutdown. Caveats: no space fission unit is flight-qualified as of mid-2026 (KRUSTY was a ground test at TRL 5); the 6 t/40 kWe goal is unmet (6.8-10 t actual designs); >100 kWe scaling is extrapolation bounded by the 13 kg/kWe in-space MW anchor; HALEU policy costs ~2x reactor mass vs HEU; all schedule/cost figures are agency claims with a history of slips.

parameters.name: Kilopower 1 kWe complete system mass
parameters.source: Smith/Mason/Palac/Gibson, 'Kilopower: Small & Affordable Fission Power Systems for Space', NTRS 20180000691
parameters.notes: Complete system (core 28 kg U-235, heat pipes, 8x125 We Stirling, radiator, light shield). Reactor thermal power 4.3 kWt.

parameters.name: Kilopower 10 kWe system mass (science/remote config, minimal shield)
parameters.source: NTRS 20180000691; Gibson et al. IEEE Aerospace 2017; core 226 kg incl 43.7 kg U-235, 43.3 kWt
parameters.notes: ~6.7 We/kg. Radiator ~9.6 m2 (Stirling option, ~500 K rejection, NTRS 20140010823).

parameters.name: Kilopower 10 kWe Mars unit mass with crew-rated shadow shield
parameters.source: Rucker, 'Solar vs. Fission Surface Power for Mars', AIAA 2016-5452 (COMPASS point design), incl. 15% MGA, shield sized <3 mR/hr at 500 m
parameters.notes: Team press statements quote ~2000 kg with shielding, less if buried. Fixed conical radiator, no deployment; 106 W keep-alive after landing; total demonstrator payload 2751 kg.

parameters.name: Kilopower design lifetime
parameters.source: NTRS 20180000691 ('1 to 10 kW continuously for 10 years or more'); Rucker 2016 presentation, NTRS 20160011275 slide 18 ('12-year Kilopower service life')
parameters.notes: Use 10 yr contractual / 12 nominal / 15 stretch. verified correction: prior source_url (nasa.gov Kilopower page) contains no lifetime figure; repointed to NTRS 20180000691. Value 12 yr (range 10-15) is unchanged.

parameters.name: Stirling converter thermal efficiency (KRUSTY measured)
parameters.source: Gibson et al., KRUSTY Nuclear Ground Test Results, AIAA P&E 2018, NTRS 20180005435 (measured 30-34%, ~50% of Carnot)
parameters.notes: Converter-level. System net efficiency is lower after thermal losses (~18-20%) and PMAD (83-90%): ~23% for FSP HP-Stirling.

parameters.name: Core temperature droop from burnup if control rod untouched (1 kWe design)
parameters.source: KRUSTY test paper, NTRS 20180005435; equivalent to ~$0.02/yr excess reactivity to hold 800 degC setpoint
parameters.notes: With periodic rod adjustment, electrical output stays constant over life; fuel burnup <1% in 10 yr. Power turn-down ratio up to ~16:1 (engines throttle to 50%, others off); reactor passively load-follows with <4% core-temp deviation for >175% load swings.

parameters.name: Fission Surface Power 40 kWe system mass (HP-Stirling gov reference, incl 20% MGA)
parameters.source: Mason et al., 'Key Design Trades for a Near-term Lunar FSP System', NETS 2025, NTRS 20250000841, Table IV
parameters.notes: NASA goal 6000 kg not met by any full design. GC-Brayton 7502 kg; fully-deployable COMPASS 2022 concept 10046 kg (+2080 kg rover); 2010 buried concept 5800 kg. Includes shield, radiators, PMAD, 1 km cable, user pallet.

parameters.name: FSP design life, no crew or robotic maintenance
parameters.source: FSP Phase 1 SOW DR-1 (2021/2022 contracts): 40 kWe for 10 years, no human intervention
parameters.notes: 2010 concept used 8 yr full-power life. Post-shutdown crew approach requires ~months of radiological cool-down.

parameters.name: FSP net thermal-to-user electrical efficiency (HP-Stirling)
parameters.source: Mason NETS 2025: 40 kWe net / 175 kWt (HP-Stirling); GC-Brayton 17% (40/230 kWt)
parameters.notes: COMPASS 2022 deployable variant achieved 18.1% end-to-end (26.1% converter x 87% PMAD, 18% reactor thermal losses).

parameters.name: FSP mass scaling law coefficient a in M[kg]=a*P[kWe]^b (HP-Stirling, incl 20% MGA)
parameters.source: This work: least-squares fit to Mason NETS 2025 Table V (10/20/40/80/100 kWe = 2.7/4.1/6.8/10.7/12.6 t)
parameters.notes: GC-Brayton fit: a=863, b=0.580; crossover ~90 kWe (Brayton lighter above). Valid 10-100 kWe; R^2>0.999 on source data.

parameters.name: FSP mass scaling law exponent b in M[kg]=a*P[kWe]^b (HP-Stirling)
parameters.source: This work: fit to Mason NETS 2025 Table V
parameters.notes: Specific mass: 270 kg/kWe @10 kWe, 170 @40, 126 @100; extrapolates to ~100 kg/kWe @200 kWe and ~59 kg/kWe @1 MWe (low confidence beyond 100 kWe).

parameters.name: MW-class fission specific mass anchor (in-space NEP design, 1.9 MWe)
parameters.source: Mason et al., NASA/TM-20210016968 (2022): 1.9 MWe HALEU SCO2-Brayton NEP <25 t incl reactor/shield/conversion/radiators/PMAD; HEU version 11 kg/kWe
parameters.notes: In-space system: 50 rem/yr shadow shield, 2-yr full-power life, 2500 m2 radiator. A Mars SURFACE MW plant with 5 rem/yr and 10-yr life is estimated 30-60 kg/kWe (speculative). HALEU costs +3500 kg vs HEU at 10 MWt (7600 vs 4100 kg reactor+shield).

parameters.name: Shadow shield mass, 40 kWe reactor, 5 rem/yr at 1 km (HP-Stirling)
parameters.source: Mason NETS 2025 Table IV: LiH/B4C/W directional shield, 36-deg included angle covering 1-km-dia outpost at 1 km
parameters.notes: GC-Brayton shield 1500 kg (larger core, higher kWt). COMPASS 2022: 1250 kg basic (LiH & W). Dose OUTSIDE shielded cone ~50 rem/yr at 1 km. Separation sensitivity: 0.5 km -> ~2700 kg (52-deg angle), 2 km -> ~700 kg (22-deg); system-mass optimum ~1.8 km, nearly flat 1-2.5 km.

parameters.name: Design crew dose limit from reactor at habitat boundary
parameters.source: FSP Phase 1 SOW DR-3 (<5 rem/yr at 1 km); same limit used in 2010 FSP and COMPASS studies; 100% occupancy assumed
parameters.notes: = 50 mSv/yr, small vs Mars natural background GCR dose (~20-30 rem/yr unshielded). Electronics limits: 10 MRad/5e14 n/cm2 at 1 m; 300 kRad/1e12 n/cm2 at 10 m.

parameters.name: Keep-out / separation distance, surface-sited shadow-shielded reactor
parameters.source: FSP SOW and all recent NASA concepts: crew habitat at 1 km within ~36-deg shielded cone
parameters.notes: Unshielded azimuths remain exclusion zone (~50 rem/yr at 1 km). Control electronics sited at 50 m need no extra shielding. 1/r^2 scaling for dose beyond design points.

parameters.name: Keep-out distance if reactor buried under regolith
parameters.source: Fission Surface Power System Initial Concept Definition, NASA/TM-2010-216772: reactor in ~2 m excavation, regolith limits dose to <5 rem/yr at 100 m
parameters.notes: Burial trades launched shield mass (1.4-2.7 t) + 1 km cable against excavation equipment and emplacement ops. 2010 buried system: 5800 kg total, 145 kg/kWe.

parameters.name: Deployed radiator area per net kWe (FSP-class, ~420 K rejection)
parameters.source: Mason NETS 2025 Table V: 40-351 m2 for 10-100 kWe (3.5-4.0 m2/kWe), two-sided vertical panels, eps 0.85 EOL
parameters.notes: Lunar 270 K design sink; Mars sink is colder on average so this is conservative, but add dust-deposition margin. Kilopower corner case: ~1 m2/kWe at ~500 K rejection with lower efficiency. Realized flux ~0.85 kWt/m2 at 403-433 K.

parameters.name: Heat rejection temperature (Stirling cold end / radiator loop)
parameters.source: Mason NETS 2025: HP-Stirling radiator 403-433 K (cold end 420 K); COMPASS 2022: 420 K inlet/370 K outlet
parameters.notes: GC-Brayton rejects 379-504 K. Pumped-water loop + Ti/H2O heat-pipe composite panels (freeze management needed on Mars nights during shutdown).

parameters.name: Autonomous startup sequence duration (cold to full power)
parameters.source: Mason NETS 2025: entire startup <8 h on 4 kWh battery + 1 kWe aux array; KRUSTY demonstrated 1.5 h bounding fast start
parameters.notes: Sequence: low-power criticality, warm heat pipes, start converters sequentially, deploy radiators with own waste heat, ramp to full. Multiple on/off cycles are a design goal.

parameters.name: Crew time required for deployment/startup/operation (nominal)
parameters.source: FSP SOW requirement: self-contained, no crew or robotic support for startup, operation, maintenance for 10 yr; deployment by robotic rover (COMPASS NETS 2022)
parameters.notes: Robotic deployment: ~2 t rover, 1-2 trips, 1 km cable unreeled to 870 kg user pallet; order days. Upper range covers crew contingency: cable hookups at user interface (safe end), site survey. Estimate for contingency hours is speculative.

parameters.name: Net electrical output degradation rate over design life (fission system)
parameters.source: Estimate: reactor output flat (constant-temperature core, rod-compensated burnup <1%/10 yr per FSP TRL data); residual term = Mars dust on radiators + converter aging
parameters.notes: Degradation is dominated by DISCRETE string failures, not drift: 4x25% strings, >=75% power after any single fault (FSP DG-5). Sink-temperature seasonal variation gives +/-2-4% power.

parameters.name: MMRTG electrical power at beginning of Mars mission
parameters.source: NASA/JPL MMRTG fact sheet; Curiosity/Perseverance flight units (design 125 We at fueling BOL, >=100 We at 14 yr)
parameters.notes: Thermal power ~2000 Wt from 4.8 kg PuO2; ~6% thermoelectric efficiency; also used directly as heat for rover thermal control.

parameters.name: MMRTG unit mass
parameters.source: NASA/JPL MMRTG fact sheet; 64.2 cm dia x 66.8 cm
parameters.notes: ~2.4 We/kg BOL - an order of magnitude worse than 10 kWe fission (6.7 We/kg) and two orders worse at 100 kWe scale. Availability limited by Pu-238 production (~1.5 kg/yr DOE target; 4.8 kg per unit).

parameters.name: MMRTG electrical power degradation rate
parameters.source: NASA/JPL MMRTG fact sheet NF-2020-05-619-HQ (BOL 110 We, est. 72 We at 17-yr design life => ~2.5%/yr compounded); MSL flight analyses ~3-4.8%/yr early-mission
parameters.notes: Sources disagree: 2026 quantitative flight-data analysis gives ~1.3-1.35%/yr effective compounded; early Curiosity data often quoted up to ~4.8%/yr. Model as P(t)=P0*(1-r)^t with r=0.018 nominal. verified correction: prior 1.8%/yr was derived from a 125->100 We / 14-yr Wikipedia curve (which actually computes to ~1.6%/yr) and is biased low vs both the official design curve (~2.5%/yr) and flight data. Keep 1.3-4.8 %/yr sensitivity range.

parameters.name: Rucker 2016 trade: solar+storage mass for crewed Mars mission (Jezero)
parameters.source: Rucker, Solar vs. Fission Surface Power for Mars, AIAA 2016-5452 / NTRS 20160002628: 22 kWe keep-alive through 120-sol tau=5 dust storm, 35 kWe clear-sky peak
parameters.notes: Presentation MEL variant: 11713 kg at Jezero (18.9N), 12679 kg at Columbus Crater (29.5S), excl. lander-to-lander PMAD (up to ~1 t/lander). UltraFlex arrays 33% cells + Li-ion 165 Wh/kg at 60% DoD; storm diffuse light 30-40% of clear direct.

parameters.name: Rucker 2016 trade: fission mass for same crewed mission (4x10 kWe Kilopower)
parameters.source: Rucker AIAA 2016-5452 abstract (4 units, 35 kWe continuous); presentation MEL: 9154 kg incl 5th spare unit + MGA + Stirling PMAD
parameters.notes: Headline: 50 kWe fission ~20% less landed mass than 35 kWe solar at Jezero Expedition 1; mass identical at any latitude/season/dust. Fission LOSES the small equatorial demo trade (2751 kg vs 1128-2425 kg solar). NASA ACR24 (Dec 2024) baselined fission as primary crewed-Mars surface power.

parameters.name: Design-basis global dust storm duration (drives solar sizing, not fission)
parameters.source: Rucker AIAA 2016-5452 study assumption: one 120-sol storm, optical depth tau=5, max wind 20 m/s
parameters.notes: Include in sim so the solar-vs-fission trade is reproducible: fission output is unchanged by tau; solar keep-alive fraction ~30-40% (diffuse). Wind loads are a structural design case for radiators/arrays, not a power source.

parameters.name: Power transmission cable mass, 1 km at +/-2800 VDC, ~43 kWe, 95% efficiency
parameters.unit: kg per 1-km link at stated design point (mass ~ L^2 at fixed efficiency)
parameters.source: Oleson et al., COMPASS 40 kWe FSP, NETS 2022: mass-optimal at +/-2800 VDC; 3 km cable = 240 kg at 2800 V vs 3300 kg at 500 V
parameters.notes: Bare optimized aluminum conductor + insulation only; no redundancy/micrometeoroid armor. NETS-2025 baseline instead uses 3 kVac/1 kHz, 4x10 kWe feeds; full user-interface pallet incl spool/PMAD = 872 kg. End-to-end converter-to-user efficiency ~78-83% (Stirling) / ~90% local PMAD (Brayton). verified correction: the kg/km unit implies incorrect linear length scaling; the same source (Oleson NETS 2022, Fig. 13) gives 240 kg for a 3 km cable (80 kg/km) because conductor area grows with L at fixed fractional loss (mass ~ L^2). The flight-like NETS-2025 1 km cable+spool assembly is 325 kg; the 45 kg is bare optimized conductor+insulation only.

parameters.name: Current NASA FSP program-of-record target (Aug 2025 directive)
parameters.source: NASA directive Aug 2025 (agency claim, ANS Nuclear Newswire 2 Sep 2025): 100 kWe-class closed-Brayton, <=15 t, launch-ready Q1 FY2030
parameters.notes: Schedule confidence LOW (repeated prior slips; SpaceNews reported fiscal challenges 2024-25). 100 kWe @ 15 t = 150 kg/kWe, consistent with scaling law. Budget: $350M FY26 request -> ~$500M/yr FY27 (whole Mars Technology line). Lunar demo first; Mars-qualified unit later. No flight-qualified space fission unit exists as of mid-2026.

parameters.name: Kilopower/KRUSTY prototype program cost (design, build, nuclear test to TRL 5)
parameters.source: NASA: '3 years and $15M' project (NTRS 20180000691); ~$20M widely cited incl DOE/NNSA contributions; DUFF precursor <$1M
parameters.notes: Prototype/ground-test cost only - NOT a flight unit price. FSP Phase 1 design contracts: 3 x $5M (2022). Flight-system development for the 40-100 kWe class estimated $0.5-2B (speculative).

isru_atmosphere.title: ISRU: Atmospheric Propellant Production
isru_atmosphere.modeling_notes: L0: produce methalox at 20 kWh/kg (range 15-25) and 23 kg plant mass per kg/day; O2-only at 15-17 kWh/kg and 19 kg/(kg/day); one Starship (1200 t) per 500 sols = 2.0 MW continuous. L1: per-sol energy ledger E = m_CO2*e_acq(method) + m_H2O*6.1 + m_O2*1.2 + m_CH4*1.5 + thermal water extraction + overhead, with seasonal atmospheric-density forcing (+/-25-30% over the CO2 cycle) scaling acquisition throughput, SOXE degradation 0.5-2%/1000 h, plant availability factor 0.85-0.95, and a heat-integration switch (Sabatier exotherm 2.87 kWh/kg CH4 can fully drive sorption capture, saving ~8-15% of plant energy). L2: sub-sol timestep with compressor flow proportional to rho_atm(t); SOXE cell V = V_Nernst + i*ASR(t) with utilization <=0.5 and coking voltage limit, m_O2 = M*I*N/(4F); Sabatier equilibrium conversion X(T,P)~0.95 at 375-400 C with exotherm routed to capture beds; electrolyzer V = 1.229 + overpotentials; cryocooler P = Q_lift*(T_rej(t)-T_c)/(T_c*eta_2L) with eta_2L = 0.10-0.20 and diurnal radiator reject temperature (night liquefaction 20-40% cheaper); tank boiloff dm/dt = (Q_leak - Q_cryo)/h_fg. Key stoichiometry: 2.75 kg CO2/kg CH4; 2.25 kg H2O produced/kg CH4; water route yields O:F 4.0 (11% O2 surplus vs Raptor 3.6, so pure water+CO2 plants need no SOXE); electrolysis is the dominant load (~6 kWh per kg propellant). Confidence: chemistry high; MOXIE flight data high; scaled plant energy/mass medium (single NASA point-design lineage); CO2 acquisition energies low-to-medium (method- and scale-dependent, span 0.15-1.7 kWh/kg); Starship numbers are company claims.

parameters.name: MOXIE nominal O2 production rate (flight)
parameters.source: Hoffman et al. 2022, Science Advances 8(35) eabp8636; Hecht et al. 2021, Space Sci. Rev. 217:9
parameters.notes: Design goal was >=6 g/hr at >=98% purity; typical runs 6-10 g/hr.

parameters.name: MOXIE peak O2 production rate (flight)
parameters.source: NASA/JPL end-of-mission release, 2023-09-06
parameters.notes: 2x original goal, at >=98% purity; limited by 4 A power supply, not the stack.

parameters.name: MOXIE cumulative O2 produced over mission
parameters.source: NASA/JPL end-of-mission release, 2023-09-06
parameters.notes: Across 16 runs, Apr 2021 - Aug 2023; ~15 h cumulative production time (derived).

parameters.name: MOXIE oxygen-producing runs completed
parameters.source: Hoffman et al. 2023, Acta Astronautica 210:547-553; NASA/JPL 2023
parameters.notes: Operated across all seasons of a full Mars year; graceful degradation (small iASR increase per run, ~0.75 -> ~0.9 ohm.cm2 through burn-in).

parameters.name: MOXIE total system power draw
parameters.source: Hecht et al. 2021, Space Sci. Rev. 217:9; NASA Mars 2020 fact sheet
parameters.notes: Breakdown: ~115 W SOXE stack (35 W electrolysis + 80 W stack heater at 800 C) + scroll compressor + avionics. Mass 17.1 kg.

parameters.name: MOXIE system-level specific energy (flight-achieved)
parameters.source: Derived: 300 W / 6-12 g/hr (Hecht 2021; NASA/JPL 2023)
parameters.notes: Small-scale penalty dominated by fixed thermal losses; thermodynamic floor is 4.91 kWh/kg. Stack-only best case 9.6 kWh/kg (2.5 g/W/day, Shahid et al. 2023 AIChE J). verified correction: the 'stack-only 9.6 kWh/kg (2.5 g/W/day, Shahid et al. 2023)' note is a stack-only derivation, not a Shahid figure; Shahid et al. benchmark MOXIE at ~0.8 g O2/W/day system-level.

parameters.name: MOXIE SOXE stack-only specific energy
parameters.source: Derived: MOXIE SOXE stack ~115 W (Hecht et al. 2021, Space Sci. Rev. 217:9) at 6-12 g/hr flight rates; cf. Shahid et al. 2023, AIChE J 69(5) e18010 (benchmarks MOXIE at ~0.8 g O2/W/day system-level)
parameters.notes: Excludes compressor and avionics; shows scaling headroom vs 30 kWh/kg system value. verified correction: the '2.5 g/W/day per Shahid et al. 2023' attribution was wrong (that paper reports 3.54 g O2/W/day for its own electrolyzer and benchmarks MOXIE at ~0.8 g O2/W/day system-level); 2.5 g/W/day is the researcher's own stack-only derivation. Value 12 kWh/kg (9.6-19.2) is unchanged.

parameters.name: Scaled MOXIE-class O2 plant power (MAV-scale, 2-3 kg/hr)
parameters.source: Hoffman et al. 2023, Acta Astronautica 210 (scaled several-hundred-x MOXIE); OxEon manned-mission stack development (4 stacks > 2.3 kg/hr)
parameters.notes: Produces ~30 t LOX in a 435-500 day window for a Mars Ascent Vehicle.

parameters.name: Scaled SOXE O2 plant specific energy (system level)
parameters.source: Derived: 25-30 kW / 2-3 kg/hr (Hoffman 2023); cross-check Kleinhenz & Paz O2-only case 34 kW / 1.97 kg/hr = 17 kWh/kg
parameters.notes: Includes CO2 acquisition and liquefaction. Use 17 for margined EMC-style design, 9-12 for optimized.

parameters.name: Thermodynamic minimum energy for CO2->CO+1/2O2
parameters.source: Thermochemistry: dH = 566 kJ per mol O2 (2CO2 -> 2CO + O2)
parameters.notes: Enthalpy basis; electric demand can be slightly below this at 800 C if Joule heat supplies TdS.

parameters.name: CO2 acquisition energy - thermal-swing sorption pump
parameters.source: TDA Research, ICES-2020-151: 0.91 kW heating/cooling for 1.51 kg CO2/hr module
parameters.notes: MOF sorbent, 4500 thermal-swing cycles demonstrated in lab; energy is heat and can be supplied by Sabatier waste heat.

parameters.name: CO2 acquisition energy - cryogenic desublimation freezer
parameters.source: Estimate: desublimation + sensible load ~620 kJ/kg at 150 K divided by COP 0.21 (20% of Carnot, per Berg TFAWS-2018 Stirling hardware, NTRS 20190000481); KSC MACDOF and Kleinhenz & Paz baseline
parameters.notes: Delivers pure CO2 (Ar/N2 rejected inherently) at high pressure on re-vaporization; NASA EMC baseline method.

parameters.name: CO2 acquisition energy - mechanical compression
parameters.source: Estimate: ideal isothermal 0.7->100 kPa is 0.055 kWh/kg; 35% single-stage efficiency measured (Acta Astronautica 2024); MOXIE scroll ~1.7 kWh/kg at gram scale
parameters.notes: Continuous and simple but passes ~4-5% Ar/N2 inerts downstream; strong scale dependence.

parameters.name: Sabatier single-pass CO2 conversion efficiency
parameters.source: NASA Sabatier System Design Study, ICES-2018 / NTRS 20180004697; PNNL microchannel reactors
parameters.notes: Ru/Al2O3 catalyst, 350-400 C, 55-520 kPa; >99% effective with recycle over H2:CO2 = 1.8-5 (ISS-heritage reactors).

parameters.name: Sabatier exothermic heat release per kg CH4
parameters.source: Thermochemistry: dH = -165.4 kJ/mol CH4
parameters.notes: High-grade waste heat (~350-400 C); can drive sorption CO2 capture (needs 1.65 kWh/kg CH4 at 0.6 kWh/kg CO2 x 2.75 kg CO2/kg CH4) or water/regolith heating. Reactor needs no net power after startup.

parameters.name: Water electrolysis specific energy per kg H2
parameters.source: Commercial PEM/alkaline electrolyzer literature (theoretical HHV 39.4; best systems 48 kWh_AC/kg); Giner cathode-feed stack used in NASA EMC model
parameters.notes: Dominant electrical load of a methalox plant: ~6.0 kWh per kg of O:F 3.6 propellant mix.

parameters.name: Water electrolysis specific energy per kg H2O processed
parameters.source: Derived: 55 kWh/kg H2 / 9 kg H2O per kg H2
parameters.notes: Yields 0.111 kg H2 + 0.889 kg O2 per kg H2O; theoretical 4.38 kWh/kg H2O.

parameters.name: O2 liquefaction specific energy on Mars (to ~90 K)
parameters.source: NASA CryoFILL (NTRS 20210023574): 2.2 kg/hr O2 requires 1.2-4 kW input; iScience 25:104323 (2022): 7.3 kW for 3 kg/hr incl. heat leaks at 10% cryocooler efficiency
parameters.notes: Depends on cryocooler second-law efficiency (10-20%) and radiator reject temperature; night operation cheaper.

parameters.name: CH4 liquefaction specific energy on Mars (to ~112 K)
parameters.source: Estimate: h_fg 511 kJ/kg + sensible, 90 K-class cryocooler at 10-20% of Carnot; same cooler class as O2 (CryoFILL philosophy)
parameters.notes: Latent heat 2.4x O2 but warmer storage temperature; CH4 is only ~22% of propellant mass so O2 liquefaction dominates plant load.

parameters.name: H2 liquefaction specific energy (to ~20 K, small scale)
parameters.source: NASA CryoFILL (NTRS 20210023574): 0.3 kg/hr H2 requires 10-30 kW input incl. para-ortho conversion
parameters.notes: Terrestrial large plants reach 10-13 kWh/kg; space-scale 20 K systems are far worse. Central cost of bring-your-own-H2 architecture.

parameters.name: Cryocooler specific power at 90 K on Mars
parameters.source: Derived from CryoFILL input-power data (5-16 W/W), iScience 2022 10%-of-Carnot assumption (23 W/W at 300 K reject), Creare 90 K/500 W reverse turbo-Brayton (NTRS 20170009150, 20180004709)
parameters.notes: Carnot floor 2.33 W/W at 90 K/300 K; Mars diurnal reject temperature swing makes night operation 20-40% cheaper.

parameters.name: Passive LH2 boiloff rate (MLI, no active cooling)
parameters.source: NASA ZBO literature (NTRS 20170006481; cryogenic fluid management reviews)
parameters.notes: 30-70% loss over a 180-300 day transit+wait without zero-boiloff cryocoolers; the reason Earth-H2 import is disfavored at human scale.

parameters.name: CH4 yield per kg Earth-supplied H2 (Sabatier only, no water recycle)
parameters.source: Stoichiometry: CO2 + 4H2 -> CH4 + 2H2O (0.5 kg H2 per kg CH4)
parameters.notes: Also produces 4.5 kg H2O per kg H2; electrolyzing and recycling that water doubles yield to 4.0 kg CH4/kg H2 plus 8.0 kg O2.

parameters.name: Total propellant leverage of Earth-supplied H2 (with water recycle + SOXE topping to O:F 3.6)
parameters.source: Stoichiometry (verified); Zubrin Mars Direct: 6 t H2 -> ~107 t methalox (18:1), The Case for Mars
parameters.notes: 12:1 without SOXE O2 topping (O:F 2.0 mix); 18.4:1 at O:F 3.6. Bought at the cost of 20 K H2 transport/storage.

parameters.name: Full methalox ISRU plant specific energy (end-to-end, incl. regolith water and liquefaction)
parameters.source: Kleinhenz & Paz, AIAA 2017-0423 / NTRS 20170001421: 52 kW for 29.7 t propellant in 480 days (Case 2, 1.3 wt% regolith water)
parameters.notes: 13.6 kWh/kg electric + 6.6 kWh/kg thermal (regolith drying; recuperable to ~35 kW total). Thermodynamic floor 3.1 kWh/kg; optimized heat-integrated designs project 10-13.

parameters.name: Full methalox ISRU plant specific mass (excl. power system)
parameters.source: Kleinhenz & Paz, AIAA 2017-0423: 1.7 t hardware for 73 kg/day product (Case 2); O2-only 0.9 t for 47 kg/day = 19
parameters.notes: Includes 15% structure + 20% growth margin and 3x modular redundancy at 40% each; expect sub-linear scaling to Starship class.

parameters.name: Starship ship full propellant load
parameters.source: SpaceX Starship V2/Block 2 spec: 1,170 t LOX + 330 t LCH4 (company claim; en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SpaceX_Starship_(spacecraft), accessed 2026-07). Range spans retired V1 through announced V3.
parameters.notes: Company claim, no independent verification; evolved from 1100 t (2017 IAC) to 1500 t (2024-2026). verified correction: 1,200 t was the retired V1 (Block 1) figure; the vehicle flying in 2025-2026 (V2/Block 2) carries 1,500 t, understating headline refueling energy/power by ~25%. Derived refuel-energy and avg-power parameters recomputed to the 1,500 t basis.

parameters.name: Raptor engine oxidizer-to-fuel mass ratio
parameters.source: SpaceX company claim (Raptor design mixture ratio; 1170/330 t split implies 3.55)
parameters.notes: O2 = 78.3% of propellant mass at 3.6. Sabatier+electrolysis chain naturally yields O:F 4.0, so water-based plants over-produce O2 by ~11% (usable for life support).

parameters.name: Total energy to produce one Starship load (1200 t liquid methalox) in 500 sols
parameters.source: Derived: 1.5e6 kg x 20.2 kWh/kg (Kleinhenz & Paz specific energy scaled); range from 15-25 kWh/kg on the 1,500 t V2 load
parameters.notes: 24.2 GWh. Feedstocks: ~600-650 t water, ~718 t CO2. Production 2400 kg/sol = 97.3 kg/hr (~8100x MOXIE peak, ~32x MAV-class plant). verified correction: recomputed from the corrected 1,500 t Starship propellant load (was 24.2 GWh at 1,200 t) -> ~30.3 GWh.

parameters.name: Average continuous power to refuel one Starship in 500 sols
parameters.source: Derived: 24.2e6 kWh / (500 sols x 24.6597 h)
parameters.notes: ~2 MW continuous electric+thermal; at PV capacity factor 0.2-0.25 implies ~8-12 MW peak / 40,000-60,000 m2 array, or several 100 kWe-class fission units. 1500 t load scales to ~2.5 MW. verified correction: recomputed from the corrected 1,500 t load and 30.3 GWh over 500 sols -> ~2.46 MW continuous (was ~1.97 MW at 1,200 t).

isru_water.title: ISRU: Water Mining & Extraction
isru_water.modeling_notes: L0 (scalar): water_rate [kg/sol] = P_thermal x 24.66 / e_specific, with e_specific = 0.6 kWh(th)/kg (Rodwell/massive ice), 1.5 (gypsum ore), 3.7 (smectite), 6-12.5 (typical regolith); add 0.1 kWh(e)/kg cleanup, 0.004/yield kWh/kg excavation for regolith routes, 0.3 kWh/t-km hauling; apply 0.6-0.8 availability. L1 (analytic daily/seasonal): ice temperature annual wave T(z,t) with skin depth ~1-2 m sets sensible-heat term; Rodwell as two-state system (startup: drill + 10-30 sols pool establishment; steady: max withdrawal = eta*P/(c_ice*dT + L_fus), collapse if exceeded; downward migration grows pump lift; abandon/redrill at ~150 m-equivalent); excavator fleet sizing N = ceil(ore_rate/(2770 kg/sol x 0.6 duty)) with traverse kinematics at 0.25 m/s and 80 kg loads; credit reactor waste heat against thermal loads on nuclear branches. L2 (sub-sol physics): CRREL-type lumped pool energy balance dT_pool/dt with melt flux from Nu = C*Ra^(1/3), conduction into ice annulus of radius ~4.5 r_pool (Lunardini & Rand 1995), and Mars-specific evaporative loss Sh = C'*Ra^(1/3) suppressed by borehole pressurization >611 Pa (JSC bell-jar experiments, NASA/TP-20205011353); regolith dryer as plug-flow ore heating with condenser recovery and recuperator effectiveness 0.3-0.7 (the lever explaining the 5.9 vs 12.5 kWh/kg disagreement between M-WIP and Kleinhenz & Paz); Monte-Carlo ore grade +/-30% (M-WIP heterogeneity). Key cautions: all Rodwell Mars numbers are simulation-based for -80 C ice (no Mars-environment full-scale demo; TRL 6 from Antarctic heritage); lab thermal-capture efficiencies at low TRL are 5-50x worse than system studies assume; SpaceX figures are company claims - parameterize per-ship reload.

parameters.name: Depth to top of massive ice, Arcadia-class mid-latitude site
parameters.source: SWIM refined maps (Morgan et al. 2025, PSJ 6:37) 0-1 m consistency band; Dundas et al. 2018 Science 359:199 scarps show ice 1-2 m below surface
parameters.notes: Highly site-variable at sub-km scale; decimeters in best Arcadia/Utopia sites (expanded secondary craters), 1-2 m at Dundas scarps. Treat as site parameter; conservative designs (RedWater) assume up to 20 m overburden.

parameters.name: Thickness of excess-ice layer, Arcadia Planitia
parameters.source: Bramson et al. 2015 GRL 42:6566 (SHARAD + crater morphology): depth-to-base mode 42 m, mean 51 +/- 18 m; Dundas 2018 scarps expose >100 m in places
parameters.notes: Volume ~1e4 km3 in Arcadia alone; effectively unlimited reservoir for settlement-scale demand (600 t/Starship = ~650 m3 of ice).

parameters.name: Ice mass fraction of mid-latitude massive ice deposits
parameters.source: Dundas et al. 2018 (massive, dust-poor scarp ice); Bramson et al. 2015 (bulk dielectric ~2.5, mostly clean ice); M-WIP 2016 reference Case A assumes 90% ice / 10% sand
parameters.notes: No in-situ ground truth yet. Purity mainly drives Rodwell sediment/sump management and melt-water dirtiness, not extraction energy (weak function above ~0.5).

parameters.name: Water content of pure gypsum (CaSO4.2H2O)
parameters.source: Stoichiometry (2x18.02/172.17); dehydration at 100-150 C confirmed in M-WIP 2016
parameters.notes: Releases water at benign 100-150 C (vs 300 C for smectite) - lowest-energy regolith route. Gypsum/polyhydrated-sulfate deposits mapped from orbit (e.g., north polar erg, Valles Marineris, Gale).

parameters.name: Bulk recoverable water fraction, gypsum-enriched regolith deposit
parameters.source: M-WIP 2016 reference Case B: 40% gypsum + minor hydrates -> 186 t ore per 16 t H2O at 425 K
parameters.notes: Hypothetical reserve description, +/-30% heterogeneity assumed; no gypsum deposit has been ground-truthed at mining scale.

parameters.name: Bulk recoverable water fraction, smectite clay-enriched deposit
parameters.source: M-WIP 2016 reference Case C: 40% smectite (avg 4 wt% water; Na-smectite ~2, Ca-smectite ~7) -> 584 t ore per 16 t H2O at 575 K
parameters.notes: Requires ~300 C process temperature. Phyllosilicates widely mapped by CRISM/OMEGA (3-8 wt% in enriched terrains per hydrated-silicate surveys).

parameters.name: Recoverable water fraction of typical (globally ubiquitous) regolith
parameters.source: MSL DAN/SAM (Gale): 1.5-3 wt% total; Kleinhenz & Paz AIAA 2017-0423: 1.3 wt% recoverable at 300 C; M-WIP Case D
parameters.notes: The 'works anywhere' fallback: 10-100x the excavated mass and 4-8x the thermal energy per kg water vs gypsum ore or ice.

parameters.name: Bulk density of undisturbed granular regolith
parameters.source: M-WIP 2016 heuristics: undisturbed granular ~2.0 +/-10% t/m3 (35% porosity); disturbed/excavated ~1.5 +/-10% t/m3 (50% porosity)
parameters.notes: Use 1500 kg/m3 for hauled (disturbed) material volume sizing; 920 kg/m3 for pure ice.

parameters.name: Perchlorate mass fraction in Mars soil
parameters.source: Hecht et al. 2009, Science 325:64 (Phoenix WCL, 0.4-0.6 wt%); corroborated by MSL SAM at Gale
parameters.notes: Mostly Mg/Ca/Na perchlorate + chlorates. Toxic (thyroid) at ppm levels -> all soil-contacted or melt water needs treatment before crew use; electrolyzers also require cleanup. Massive glacial ice expected much cleaner but not measured.

parameters.name: RASSOR 2.0 excavator mass
parameters.source: Mueller et al., RASSOR 2.0, ASCE Earth & Space 2016, NTRS 20210011366
parameters.notes: Counter-rotating bucket drums give near-zero net excavation force (works in 0.38 g). Reference excavator in M-WIP and Kleinhenz & Paz sizing.

parameters.name: RASSOR 2.0 regolith payload per trip
parameters.source: Mueller et al., RASSOR 2.0, ASCE 2016, NTRS 20210011366 (2 x 40 kg drums)
parameters.notes: Payload/vehicle mass ratio 1.2 - unusually high for planetary rovers.

parameters.name: RASSOR 2.0 excavation rate (minimum demonstrated design point)
parameters.source: Mueller et al. ASCE 2016: >=2.7 t per 24 h day (=2,770 kg/sol); 0.38 kg vehicle mass per (kg/h) rate
parameters.notes: Earth prototype in simulant; apply 60% duty cycle (M-WIP battery/recharge assumption) for fleet sizing. One RASSOR moves ~1.8 t/sol of ore at 60% duty.

parameters.name: Specific excavation energy, granular regolith (RASSOR-class)
parameters.source: Mueller et al. ASCE 2016: 4 W per (kg/h) excavation rate = 4 Wh/kg
parameters.notes: Negligible vs extraction heat: at 8.6 wt% yield ~0.05 kWh/kg water, at 1.3 wt% ~0.31 kWh/kg water. Icy/ice-cemented ground is NOT excavatable by RASSOR - needs drilling (Rodwell) or aggressive cutters; use upper range +50-100% for duricrust.

parameters.name: Excavator traverse speed
parameters.source: M-WIP 2016 RASSOR key characteristics (25 cm/s)
parameters.notes: Drives trips/sol = f(haul distance); at 1 km one-way a round trip is ~2.2 h + load/dump.

parameters.name: Practical max ore-haul distance, single-digit excavator fleet
parameters.source: M-WIP 2016 excavation/travel analysis: gypsum case feasible to ~3 km with 2 RASSOR-class excavators in 480-sol campaign; typical regolith excavator-limited even at 100 m
parameters.notes: Design rule: co-locate plant and mine; haul or pump water, not low-grade ore.

parameters.name: Surface transport specific energy (regolith/water hauling)
parameters.source: Estimate: rolling resistance physics, Crr 0.15-0.25 loose regolith, g=3.71 m/s2, drivetrain efficiency ~0.5; consistent with M-WIP RASSOR traverse assumptions
parameters.notes: E = Crr*g*d/eta per kg hauled. Slow rovers with high hotel loads (MSL-style) can be 5x worse; purpose-built haulers approach the physics limit.

parameters.name: Theoretical energy to melt massive ice (from -80 C)
parameters.source: Thermodynamics: cp_ice ~1.9 kJ/kg.K avg x 80 K + 334 kJ/kg fusion = ~486 kJ/kg; NASA JSC ice drilling assessment chart ~140 kWh per 1000 kg for -80 C ice
parameters.notes: Lower bound of all routes. -20 C ice: ~0.10 kWh/kg. Rodwell practice runs 3-5x this due to conductive losses into surrounding ice and evaporative losses at the pool surface.

parameters.name: Theoretical energy to sublime ice (vapor-transport extraction routes)
parameters.source: Thermodynamics: L_sub = 2838 kJ/kg + sensible heat from ~210 K = ~2940 kJ/kg
parameters.notes: Applies to open-pit volatilization, auger dryers venting vapor, and any route that boils/evaporates rather than melts. Condenser heat (~2.5 MJ/kg) is recoverable in principle (recuperation is the big design lever).

parameters.name: Practical thermal energy to extract water from icy regolith (20-40 wt% ice, sublimation/vapor route incl. matrix heating)
parameters.source: Thermo model ((1-y)/y*c_reg*dT + c_ice*dT + L_sub) bracketed by NASA ISRU studies (Kleinhenz NTRS 20180005542 program targets; Hoffman JSC assessment); lower bound assumes partial heat recuperation
parameters.notes: Lab capture efficiencies remain poor at low TRL (Linne & Kleinhenz 2016 breadboard: 17% of heater energy into soil; LUWEX lunar demo ~1-2% overall). Multiply by 1.2-2.0 for near-term systems.

parameters.name: Mars Rodwell water production at 10 kW thermal (CRREL simulation, -80 C ice)
parameters.source: Hoffman et al.: CRREL Mars sims 120-400 L/day at 2-10 kW(th); 100 gal/day (379 kg/day) balances at ~10 kW. NASA/TP-20205011353 & JSC ice drilling assessment
parameters.notes: Scales ~linearly with thermal power in this range (see specific energy param). Withdrawal > melt rate collapses (refreezes) the well; overpowering wastes heat growing an unused pool. Wells restartable after deliberate freeze-down (South Pole/Camp Century heritage). Antarctic anchors: South Pole station well ~2,005 t/yr; IceCube wells ~1,140 t/season.

parameters.name: Rodwell steady-state specific thermal energy (Mars, -80 C ice)
parameters.source: Derived from Hoffman/CRREL simulation points: 2 kW/120 L/day = 0.40; 10 kW/379-400 L/day = 0.60-0.63 kWh/kg (NASA/TP-20205011353; JSC ice-mining assessment 2016)
parameters.notes: THERMAL, not electric - can be reactor waste heat. Excludes drilling, pumping (mgh ~0.0002 kWh/kg per 150 m lift - negligible), cleanup, and startup transient (~10-30 sols at full power before first water; restart cost similar). Warmer mid-latitude ice (210-230 K at depth) cuts this 20-40%. Borehole must be sealed/pressurized >611 Pa to suppress evaporation/boiling.

parameters.name: Design borehole depth for Mars Rodwell through overburden into ice
parameters.source: RedWater design envelope: drill through up to ~20-25 m overburden (Mellerowicz et al. 2022, New Space, doi:10.1089/space.2021.0057); pool cavity forms 2.5-5 m tall, 5-10 m dia below that (Hoffman CRREL sims)
parameters.notes: Well migrates downward as ice is consumed; South Pole wells abandoned at ~150 m pump-lift depth after ~a decade. For sim: new borehole per ~10^3-10^4 t withdrawn or model lift growth.

parameters.name: Soil-processing thermal energy per kg water, gypsum-enriched ore (8.6 wt%, 425 K)
parameters.source: M-WIP 2016 Case B: ~2 kW for 33 kg H2O/sol; theory floor ~1.15 kWh/kg (matrix heating + dehydration enthalpy)
parameters.notes: Cheapest regolith route and lowest process temperature (100-150 C) - also lowest corrosion risk (no HCl/H2S evolution below ~400 C).

parameters.name: Soil-processing thermal energy per kg water, smectite ore (2.7 wt%, 575 K)
parameters.source: M-WIP 2016 Case C: ~5 kW for 33 kg H2O/sol; theory floor ~3.1 kWh/kg
parameters.notes: Dominated by heating 36 kg of matrix per kg of water to 300 C; recuperator effectiveness is the key design lever.

parameters.name: Soil-processing thermal energy per kg water, typical regolith (1.3 wt%, 575 K)
parameters.source: Range spans M-WIP Case D2 (~8 kW/33 kg/sol = 5.9) and Kleinhenz & Paz AIAA 2017-0423 (17 kW thermal for 1.36 kg/h = 12.5); disagreement from recuperation and yield assumptions
parameters.notes: Sources genuinely disagree 2x - carry the range, not the mean. Kleinhenz & Paz full-system context: 52 kW / 1.7 t total for MAV-scale plant; heat could come from fission waste heat, collapsing the electric penalty.

parameters.name: TRIDENT 1-m-class rotary-percussive drill mass
parameters.source: Honeybee Robotics/NASA (VIPER, PRIME-1): <20 kg, rotary and percussive actuators 200 W each, 200 N max weight-on-bit (Zacny et al., PSJ doi:10.3847/PSJ/ae0b51; Glass et al. 2024, NTRS 20240000585)
parameters.notes: Bite-sampling in 10 cm (down to 2 cm in hard material) increments. Flown hardware class (PRIME-1/IM-2, 2025). Scale reference for prospecting and pilot-hole drilling; RedWater coiled-tube drill is the production-scale analog.

parameters.name: Drilling energy per meter of borehole (regolith to ice-cemented ground)
parameters.source: Estimate: TRIDENT actuator power (~100-400 W draw) at field penetration rates - loose soil ~1+ m/h, massive ice-cemented material as slow as 0.16 m/h (7 cm/27 min, Haughton Crater, Glass et al. 2024, NTRS 20240000585)
parameters.notes: Energy trivial vs extraction; time and bit-life dominate. For sim: drilling rate parameter matters more than energy - use 0.15-1 m/h in ice-cemented ground.

parameters.name: Water cleanup energy (filtration + perchlorate removal + deionization, electrolysis-grade)
parameters.source: Estimate: terrestrial ion-exchange/biocatalytic perchlorate removal is <0.01 kWh/kg but consumable-limited; vapor-compression distillation bounds the robust route at ~0.1-0.2 kWh/kg
parameters.notes: No integrated Mars-water treatment demo has published end-to-end energy. Regolith-dryer condensate dirtier than Rodwell melt (volatilized Cl/S species above ~400 C). Perchlorate is also an O2 resource (brine electrolysis concepts) but no baseline credits it.

parameters.name: Water consumed per kg methane produced (Sabatier + electrolysis, net)
parameters.source: Stoichiometry: net 2 H2O + CO2 -> CH4 + 2 O2; 2x18.02/16.04 = 2.246. Cross-check: Kleinhenz & Paz require 15,701 kg H2O for 6,978 kg CH4 (ratio 2.25)
parameters.notes: Co-produces 3.99 kg O2 per kg CH4 - more than Raptor's ~3.5-3.8 O/F needs, so no additional water is required for oxidizer in an all-Sabatier plant (~14% O2 surplus for life support/losses).

parameters.name: Water required to fully refuel one Starship on Mars
parameters.source: SpaceX Starship Block 2/V3 specs: ~1,500-1,600 t ship propellant, ~330-350 t CH4 (en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SpaceX_Starship_(spacecraft); payloadspace.com/what-to-expect-from-starship-v3) x 2.246 kg H2O/kg CH4
parameters.notes: Range covers propellant loads 1,100-1,500 t across Starship versions and O/F 3.5-3.8. Over one synodic period (~640 sols) this is ~940 kg/sol = 2.4 Rodwells at 10 kW(th) each, or ~24 kW(th) continuous at 0.6 kWh/kg - water extraction is the logistics driver of propellant ISRU, not the energy driver (electrolysis/liquefaction dominate energy). verified correction: prior 600 t anchored to the superseded V1 1,200 t load; flying Block 2/V3 (1,500-1,600 t, ~330-350 t CH4) implies ~740-790 t water/reload, above the old 660 t max. Downstream per-sol rates (~940 kg/sol -> ~1,160 kg/sol) are ~25% higher.

food.title: Food & Crop Production
food.modeling_notes: Food splits into (1) resupplied prepackaged food (well-characterized ISS flight data: 1.83-2.39 kg/CM-day incl. ~0.27 kg packaging, 3037 kcal/CM-day demand, <=5 yr shelf life is an open gap) and (2) in-situ bioregenerative crops (soft numbers, all from NASA's Biomass Production Chamber 1987-1998). The crop trade is governed by radiation-use efficiency (RUE, g edible dw per mol PAR), harvest index, and LED photon efficacy. Lighting energy per calorie is set by 1/(RUE_edible x efficacy x delivery x caloric_density) and is INDEPENDENT of DLI (DLI only trades area vs. time). Central result: potato (high HI 0.70, RUE_edible 0.44) costs ~0.066 kWh/kcal vs. wheat ~0.17 and soybean ~0.18 -- potato/sweet-potato are ~3x cheaper per calorie, so a Mars menu should be tuber-biased with grain/legumes as nutrition supplements. Worked example (50% of one crew member's calories from potato+wheat): ~21-27 m2 and ~5.5-9 kW electrical (~135-224 kWh/sol), with wheat driving ~70% of the power. Full balanced-diet closure ~40-60 m2 and ~10-20 kW per person. CRITICAL CAUTIONS: (a) BVAD's chamber power of 2.6 kW/m2 is HPS-lamp-based and obsolete -- derive lighting from LED efficacy instead, this is the biggest error in legacy estimates; (b) BVAD Table 4-89 wheat light (115 mol/m2-d) is inconsistent with its own nominal yield -- use Wheeler RUE; (c) all BPC data are ~1 g Earth with elevated CO2 (1000-1200 ppm) and are optimistic upper bounds for Mars partial-gravity surface systems; (d) crop transpiration (~4 L/m2-d) is recovered as clean condensate and is a water-recovery benefit, not just a load. Fidelity tiers: L0 single scalars (Ybar~15 g edible dw/m2-d, ebar~0.10 kWh/kcal, ~45 m2 & ~10-15 kW per CM full closure); L1 per-crop analytic with edible_prod = RUE_edible x DLI x f_abs, staggered planting, seasonal Mars insolation, nutrient/water loops; L2 sub-sol physics (light-response photosynthesis, respiration, Penman-Monteith transpiration, coupled O2/CO2/water/thermal exchange with the habitat, stochastic pathogen/VOC/equipment failures on cycle-length recovery).

parameters.name: Crew metabolic energy requirement
parameters.source: NASA BVAD NASA/TP-2015-218570 Rev2, Eq. 3-2 (EER) and Tables 4-57/4-61 (12.707 MJ/CM-d)
parameters.notes: 12.707 MJ/CM-day for the average astronaut via the Estimated Energy Requirement equation. Older CELSS studies use 2500-3000 kcal.

parameters.name: Prepackaged food mass, as-shipped (incl. intrinsic water, incl. packaging)
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Table 4-47 (Perchonok 2002 lower; Douglas 2017 max 6-month rate upper)
parameters.notes: ISS flight value; 42-53% water content. Packaging is a separate line item (~0.27 kg/CM-d).

parameters.name: Food dry mass (reference)
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Table 4-47 (MSIS 1995) and Table 4-60 (Levri)
parameters.notes: Dehydrated/dry basis, excludes intrinsic and rehydration water.
