parameters.name: Food packaging mass
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Table 4-40 (Ewert 2017); upper from 2.36 kg total with 17% packaging
parameters.notes: Packaging is ~13-17% of prepackaged-food upmass; becomes trash mass.

parameters.name: Required prepackaged-food shelf life for Mars
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Sec. 4.5.1 (Cooper et al. 2011)
parameters.notes: Current ISS foods degrade (vitamin/quality loss) before 5 yr; a genuine open technology gap and a driver toward in-situ crops for multi-year stays.

parameters.name: Edible dry-matter caloric density (staple-crop average)
parameters.source: MELiSSA/Frontiers 2023 (avg of bread wheat, durum wheat, potato, soybean = 4000 kcal/kg dw)
parameters.notes: Staples (wheat/potato/rice) ~3.7-4.0; soybean ~4.9; peanut ~5.5; lettuce ~2.5-3.0. Ideal edible composition 70% carb / 20% protein / 10% lipid.

parameters.name: White potato edible productivity (dry basis)
parameters.source: Wheeler et al. 2008 best BPC (18.4; 20.8 with transplant); U. Wisconsin >30; BVAD Table 4-90 nominal 21.06
parameters.notes: Highest edible dry-mass productivity of any candidate crop; the calorie workhorse.

parameters.name: White potato radiation-use efficiency, edible biomass
parameters.source: Wheeler et al. 2008 (0.44 measured, 0.50 with transplant; U. Wisconsin >0.80)
parameters.notes: RUE_total 0.64. Sets lighting energy per calorie; use 0.44 (conservative) to 0.5-0.64 (optimistic).

parameters.name: White potato harvest index
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Table 4-90 (0.70); Wheeler 2008 measured ~0.68; Tibbitts CELSS 0.80
parameters.notes: High HI is why potato dominates calorie-per-area and calorie-per-kWh.

parameters.name: Wheat (grain) edible productivity (dry basis)
parameters.source: Wheeler et al. 2008 measured 11.3 (15.8 with VOC scrub to HI 0.40); BVAD Table 4-90 nominal 20.0
parameters.notes: BPC HI was depressed to 0.29 by ethylene; scrubbing VOCs recovers HI 0.40. Total-biomass productivity 39.6 g dw/m2-d.

parameters.name: Wheat radiation-use efficiency, edible (grain) biomass
parameters.source: Wheeler et al. 2008 (0.17 measured, 0.24 with VOC scrub, 0.30 potential); RUE_total 0.59
parameters.notes: Low edible RUE (grain HI 0.4) makes wheat ~2.6x more power-costly per calorie than potato under LEDs.

parameters.name: Wheat harvest index
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Table 4-90 (0.40); Wheeler 2008 observed 0.29 (ethylene-depressed)
parameters.notes: Ethylene/VOC accumulation in closed chambers cut BPC wheat HI to 0.29; scrubbing restores ~0.40.

parameters.name: Sweet potato edible productivity (dry basis)
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Table 4-90 (HI 0.60)
parameters.notes: Highest BVAD edible productivity; DLI 28 mol/m2-d, 12 h photoperiod, 85 d cycle. Excellent calorie crop.

parameters.name: Soybean edible productivity (dry basis)
parameters.source: Wheeler et al. 2008 (6.0 measured, 6.9 with transplant); BVAD Table 4-90 nominal 4.54
parameters.notes: Primary protein/oil crop; RUE_edible only 0.16 g/mol so it is power-expensive but nutritionally essential.

parameters.name: Soybean radiation-use efficiency, edible biomass
parameters.source: Wheeler et al. 2008 (0.16 measured, 0.19 transplant); RUE_total 0.43
parameters.notes: Lowest edible RUE of the staples; caloric density ~4.9 kcal/g dw partly offsets this.

parameters.name: Lettuce edible productivity (dry basis)
parameters.source: Wheeler et al. 2008 (7.1 measured, 11.8 with transplant, RUE 0.70); BVAD Table 4-90 6.57
parameters.notes: Fast 28-30 d cycle, HI 0.90, but ~2.5-3 kcal/g dw and mostly water; a morale/vitamin crop, not calories.

parameters.name: Tomato edible productivity (dry basis)
parameters.source: Wheeler et al. 2008 (9.8 measured, 11.4 transplant); BVAD Table 4-90 10.43; RUE_edible 0.25
parameters.notes: Salad crop; 94% water fresh; HI 0.45.

parameters.name: Crop transpiration / water uptake (mixed-crop average)
parameters.source: Wheeler et al. 2008 (3.96 avg condensed); BVAD Table 4-91 per-crop (lettuce 2.1 ... wheat 11.79)
parameters.notes: Recovered as near-distilled condensate on cooling coils; ~100% recyclable to nutrient solution or potable loop. Wheat is a large water user (11.8 L/m2-d).

parameters.name: LED horticultural photon efficacy (2026 planning value)
parameters.source: Kusuma, Pattison & Bugbee 2020 (ceiling 3.4 white+red / 4.1 blue+red); industry 2023-24 fixtures 2.5-3.5
parameters.notes: Diode electrical->photon efficiency: blue 0.93, white 0.76, red 0.81. Water/humidity optics cut ~10%.

parameters.name: Light delivery efficiency (PPF at canopy / PPF emitted)
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Table 4-86 (Sager 1999 low; Sager 2006 high)
parameters.notes: Combine with efficacy: usable canopy PAR per electrical J = efficacy x delivery. BPC reflectors achieved only 0.10-0.15.

parameters.name: Electrical energy per mol PAR photons delivered to canopy
parameters.source: Derived: 1/(efficacy x delivery); efficacy 3.0 umol/J, delivery 0.8 (range from 2.5-3.5 umol/J)
parameters.notes: At LED output (delivery=1): 0.079-0.111 kWh/mol. This is the fundamental electricity-to-photons conversion.

parameters.name: LED electrical energy per food kcal, potato
parameters.source: Derived: 1/(RUE_edible x efficacy x delivery x caloric_density); potato RUE 0.44, 3.0 umol/J, 0.8, 4.0 kcal/g
parameters.notes: Lighting only, excludes ~25% pump/fan/thermal overhead. Optimistic 0.045 (RUE 0.64), conservative 0.075 (efficacy 2.5).

parameters.name: LED electrical energy per food kcal, wheat
parameters.source: Derived: wheat RUE_edible 0.17 (0.24 opt), efficacy 3.0 umol/J, delivery 0.8, 4.0 kcal/g
parameters.notes: ~2.6x potato's cost per calorie; drives the potato-over-grain design conclusion.

parameters.name: Plant growth chamber equivalent system mass per growing area
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Table 4-88 (Drysdale 1999b); lower bound = structure+root-zone excl. HPS lamps/ballasts
parameters.notes: Includes 20 kg/m2 crop structure + 36.8 kg/m2 root-zone water/nutrients + 31 kg/m2 HPS lamps+ballasts (LEDs lighter). Aeroponics reduces root-zone mass.

parameters.name: Fertilizer salt for full-diet crop production
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Sec. 4.14 (Lunn et al. 2017), for 2500 kcal/person/day full diet
parameters.notes: >50% recyclable from inedible biomass (composting/bioreactor) and processed urine; scales with closure fraction.

parameters.name: Growing area for 100% caloric closure (balanced diet)
parameters.source: Derived from Wheeler productivities (3037 kcal / 4 kcal/g / ~15 g edible dw/m2-d); consistent with CELSS/ICES literature
parameters.notes: Potato-only idealized ~41 m2; balanced nutrition (protein/oil crops) pushes to 40-60 m2. Excludes ~25-50% crew-access aisle area.

parameters.name: Electrical power for 100% caloric closure (balanced diet, LED)
parameters.source: Derived: 3037 kcal x ~0.10-0.17 kWh/kcal mix / 24.66 h x 1.25 overhead (LED 3.0 umol/J)
parameters.notes: Dominated by lighting. Potato-heavy mix lower (~8-10 kW); grain/legume-heavy higher. The single largest life-support power line item if food is grown.

parameters.name: Growing area, 50% calories from potato+wheat (worked example)
parameters.source: Derived (Sec. 8): Wheeler RUE/productivity; 27.1 m2 as-measured, 21.1 m2 optimized
parameters.notes: Potato ~9-10 m2, wheat ~12-17 m2. Wheat needs more area per calorie despite equal calorie split.

parameters.name: Electrical power, 50% calories from potato+wheat (worked example)
parameters.source: Derived (Sec. 8): 135 kWh/sol optimized to 224 kWh/sol as-measured, incl. +25% overhead; LED 3.0 umol/J
parameters.notes: Wheat contributes ~70% of the power despite half the calories. Range spans efficacy 2.5-3.5 umol/J and as-measured vs optimized crops.

parameters.name: Mars surface usable daily light integral (favorable latitudes)
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Sec. 4.14.1.1 (Clawson 2006)
parameters.notes: Mars receives ~43% of Earth's solar irradiance; up to ~30 mol/m2-d at some latitudes for much of the year, but dust storms can suppress it drastically. Enough for hybrid natural+LED but not sole-source for staples.

reliability.title: Reliability, Maintenance & Spares
reliability.modeling_notes: Core demand model (NASA-standard): per-ORU annual failure rate lambda = duty_cycle x quantity x K_factor x 8760/MTBF (ICES-2025-127); failures over window T are Poisson(lambda*T) with cold-spare POS(n) = sum_{x=0..n} (lambda T)^x e^{-lambda T}/x!; system POS is the product over item types, so per-item POS must be ~POS_sys^(1/N) (~0.9999 for 0.99 over 150 items). Carry epistemic uncertainty by drawing lambda from a lognormal with error factor EF = p95/p50 (sigma_ln = ln(EF)/1.645; gamma-Poisson/negative-binomial equivalent per ICES-2024-95): EF~2 for ISS-heritage hardware, 5-10 for new fluid-handling designs; this uncertainty doubles-to-triples spares mass at fixed confidence (12-17 t vs 6 t for a 4-crew/1,100-d transit hab). Allocate spares by greedy marginal delta-POS/delta-kg (EMAT logic). L0: spares upmass = 10% of supported dry mass per Earth-year (5% optimistic, 28% conservative), maintenance crew time 6.0 h/CM-week, availability 0.99. L1: per-subsystem NB demand + POS optimization + crew-time POS check against work-opportunity time (flag weeks where maintenance demand exceeds available hours). L2: per-ORU renewal processes - exponential for electronics (ISS CFR practice), Weibull beta~1.8 rotating machinery, beta~1.3 valves, deterministic life/throughput clocks for beds/filters/bladders, infant-mortality beta~0.8 for new units; beta-factor CCF ~0.05 across identical units; cannibalization queue (p~0.3 recovery); ISM job shop covering ~33% of items at 0.01-10 kg/h cutting maintenance mass ~28-34%; commonality cuts spares count 33-50%. Crew repair events: R&R time median 3 h (1-19 h) + gather/stow + Mars ground-loop overhead ~0.5-1.5 h. Assumptions to stress-test: dormant systems don't fail; repairs restore good-as-new; failures independent. POS is an upper bound on reliability - CCF and unknown-unknowns historically dominate (OGA cell stack, CDRA dust, UPA CaSO4). Convert MTBF hours to sols by /24.6597; multiply per-Earth-year rates by 1.881 for per-Mars-year (668.6 sols).

parameters.name: ISS scheduled maintenance crew time per crewmember-week (flight data 2017-2023)
parameters.source: Lynch et al., ICES-2024-132, Table 4 (OPTimIS dataset, Oct 2017-Dec 2023)
parameters.notes: Corrective + preventive scheduled maintenance. Adjacent categories: Routine Ops 4.5, Logistics 2.3 h/CM-wk. Use ~15% of a 40 h work-week as L0 maintenance fraction; add Mars comm-delay overhead.

parameters.name: ISS ECLSS-only maintenance and repair crew time, station-wide
parameters.source: Owens et al., AIAA 2017-5124, citing Bagdigian et al., ICES-2015-094; design documents predicted ~1 h/week
parameters.notes: Actual exceeded design estimate (~1 h/wk) by >10x; Russell & Klaus 2007 found +60.4% in analyzed categories. Anchor for regenerative-ECLSS maintenance burden per ~6-crew outpost; apply as pessimism multiplier on bottom-up estimates.

parameters.name: Fraction of maintenance crew time that is corrective (vs preventive/scheduled)
parameters.source: Estimate: based on Russell & Klaus 2007 ISS Increments 4-8 category breakdown (35.3% of ECLSS maintenance time on air/waste repair) and ISS ops practice
parameters.notes: No clean modern public split; ISS planning emphasizes preventive. Treat corrective portion as stochastic (scales with realized failures), preventive as deterministic clock.

parameters.name: Fraction of ECLSS ORUs whose flight failure rate exceeded pre-flight estimate
parameters.source: Jones, ICES-2019-14 (14 of 20 ECLSS ORUs with changed estimates were worse; MADS vs ~8 yr flight data)
parameters.notes: ECLSS/fluid-handling specific. Contrast with MADS-wide Bayesian updates (see fraction_madswide_mtbf_adjusted_down).

parameters.name: Fraction of all ISS Crit-1/2 components whose Bayesian-updated MTBF decreased
parameters.source: Stromgren et al., AIAA 2016-5307 (MADS updates through June 2016; ~3% ended at <=0.25x initial MTBF; ~85% improved, 14% of those >=10x)
parameters.notes: Use as station-wide prior; the small worse-tail dominates system risk (asymmetry). ECLSS classes should be biased toward the Jones result.

parameters.name: Worst-case observed flight-to-predicted failure rate multiplier (ECLSS ORUs)
parameters.source: Jones, ICES-2019-14 (four UPA ORUs at 7.5-22x; seven other ORUs at 1.9-6.3x)
parameters.notes: Tail anchor for the epistemic failure-rate distribution of new fluid-handling hardware.

parameters.name: K-factor (induced-failure multiplier on intrinsic failure rate), median across ECLSS ORUs
parameters.source: Chen, ICES-2025-127, Appendix Tables 2-11 (ISS MADS-derived; e.g., WPA pump/separator 3.26, avionics air assy 3.2, most items 1.0-1.4)
parameters.notes: Annual failure rate = duty_cycle x quantity x K x 8760 / MTBF. Captures crew error, environment, false alarms, cross-system damage.

parameters.name: Default lognormal error factor (p95/p50) on failure-rate estimates for new hardware
parameters.source: Estimate: EF definition per Anderson et al. AIAA 2012-5320; magnitude anchored to Jones ICES-2019-14 flight ratios (1.9-22x) and Stromgren 2016 MTBF-change distribution
parameters.notes: sigma_ln = ln(EF)/1.645. Use EF~2 for ISS-heritage mature hardware, 5-10 for new-design fluid handling on first Mars deployment.

parameters.name: Accumulated operating time (multiple of MTBF) needed for 95% confidence in MTBF estimate
parameters.source: Stromgren et al., AIAA 2016-5307 (3x for 95% confidence, ~5x for 99%)
parameters.notes: Drives value of pre-deployment testing and lunar/ISS heritage in reducing EF.

parameters.name: UPA Fluids Control & Pump Assembly MTBF, flight-derived
parameters.source: Jones, ICES-2019-14, Table 3 (flight data; MADS pre-flight estimate was 22,759 h). ICES-2025-127 MADS now lists 22,759 h post-redesign
parameters.notes: Worst ECLSS actor: alone drove UPA insufficient-spares probability to 0.983 over 450 d. 1,000 h ~= 41 sols continuous.

parameters.name: UPA Distillation Assembly MTBF, flight-derived
parameters.source: Jones, ICES-2019-14, Table 3 (MADS estimate 41,376 h; CaSO4 precipitation from microgravity bone-loss urine chemistry was root cause)
parameters.notes: Classic unknown-unknown CCF example. PCPA 3,000 h, SPA 4,000 h from same table.

parameters.name: OGA recirculation Pump ORU MTBF, flight-derived
parameters.source: Jones, ICES-2019-14, Table 2 (MADS estimate 189,433 h -> flight 35,000 h, 5.4x worse)
parameters.notes: OGA cell-stack degradation (design-oversight CCF chain) dominated OGA ops impact; cured by ACTEX deionizing bed with ~11,000 h replacement interval.

parameters.name: CDRA valves/heaters effective MTBF (problem components, early flight experience)
parameters.source: Review: 'Toward sustainable living in space' (2025), summarizing ISS heritage: CDRA valves and heaters 800-1,500 h; sorbent dust/check-valve damage per Jones ICES-2019-14
parameters.notes: Post-redesign 2025 MADS lists CDRA selector valve 117,000 h, blower/precooler 129,700 h - use pair (early vs mature) to bracket new-deployment risk.

parameters.name: Median MTBF across mature ISS ECLSS ORUs (2025 MADS)
parameters.source: Chen, Rivas & Rangel, ICES-2025-127, Appendix Tables 2-11 (full ORU population; ~1.3e5 h if restricted to active process ORUs with MTBF <= 1e6 h)
parameters.notes: Median ~1e5 h ~= 4,055 sols. Pumps/rotating machinery cluster at 5,000-20,000 h per 2025 review. verified correction: prior 1e5 h median holds only for the active/process-ORU subset; across the full listed ORU population (which the 8,180-2e8 h range implies) the median is ~1.8e5 h. Min (MCA 8,180 h) and max (passive vents 2e8 h) endpoints are correct.

parameters.name: Median crew time to remove & replace a major ECLSS ORU (excl. gather/stow)
parameters.source: Chen, ICES-2025-127, Table 1 (R&R times: most 2-5 h; OGA hydrogen dome 14 h; WPA gas separator group 19.25 h) and MTTR columns (0.3-17.7 h)
parameters.notes: Add gather/stow + diagnosis + Mars ground-loop (2x ~22 min light time + deliberation) overhead of ~0.5-1.5 h per event.

parameters.name: Target probability of sufficiency (spares) per critical system, Mars mission
parameters.source: EMC practice: Stromgren AIAA 2016-5307 (0.99 DSH reliability spares case), Owens/NASA POS framing ICES-2024-95; ISS historical POS/confidence targets 85-99.5% (Anderson AIAA 2012-5320)
parameters.notes: With ~150 repairable item types, per-item POS must be ~0.9999 (0.99^(1/150)). Crew-survival-critical functions should target 0.999+ with dissimilar backup.

parameters.name: ISS spares inventory maintained on orbit (USOS)
parameters.source: Owens & de Weck, AIAA 2016-5394 (>13,000 kg on orbit; ~18,000 kg staged on ground; >95% of corrective spares expected never used)
parameters.notes: Range max = total staged posture incl. ground. Demonstrates the LEO-with-resupply baseline a Mars base cannot replicate.

parameters.name: Spares mass for 4-crew, 1,100-day Mars transit habitat at R=0.99 (deterministic MTBFs)
parameters.source: Stromgren et al., AIAA 2016-5307 (EMAT, EMC deep-space habitat critical systems incl. ECLSS, TCS, EPS, C&DH, comm, water recovery)
parameters.notes: THE anchor for 4-6 crew Mars hab sparing. ~2,000 kg per Earth-year, ~10%/yr of a 20 t habitat dry mass.

parameters.name: Spares mass to hold 0.99 reliability at 95th-pct confidence with MTBF epistemic uncertainty
parameters.source: Stromgren et al., AIAA 2016-5307 (>12,000 kg low-uncertainty bound, >17,000 kg high-uncertainty); Owens & de Weck AIAA 2016-5394 (17,232 kg for median P(LoC contrib)=1e-3, +4,124 kg per further 10x risk reduction)
parameters.notes: Uncertainty roughly doubles-to-triples the deterministic spares mass. This is the planning value for first Mars missions.

parameters.name: Mars-planning annual spares fraction of supported dry mass (POS 0.99, no resupply)
parameters.source: Derived: 5,984 kg (deterministic) to 17,232 kg (with uncertainty) per AIAA 2016-5307/5394, over 1,100 d against ~20,000 kg habitat dry mass (ICES-2015-288 typical)
parameters.notes: L0 scalar: 0.10/yr; 0.05/yr is the Salyut/Mir/ISS historical heuristic (Larson & Pranke via ICES-2015-288, valid only with resupply); 0.20-0.28 conservative for new hardware at high confidence. Multiply by 1.881 for per-Mars-year rates.

parameters.name: Spares as fraction of all Earth-delivered mass, growing settlement at month 130
parameters.source: Do et al., Acta Astronautica 120 (2016) (Mars One assessment, ISS-derived failure rates, crews added every 26 months)
parameters.notes: Spares come to dominate settlement logistics; range reflects sensitivity to commonality/shared-spares (Jones ICES-2015-044: sharing could cut required spares ~50%).

parameters.name: Reduction in required spares count from parts commonality/reconfigurability across elements
parameters.source: Siddiqi & de Weck, J. Spacecraft & Rockets 44(1) 2007 (Mars surface mission case study; quoted in Jones ICES-2015-044)
parameters.notes: Same availability with 33-50% fewer spares. Caveat: commonality concentrates common-cause/design-defect exposure for unproven designs (Jones ICES-2017-83).

parameters.name: Fraction of a failed ORU's function recoverable via cannibalization/sub-ORU repair
parameters.source: Estimate: ORU-level replacement discards items with useful life (AIAA 2017-5124); shared/cold-spare pooling across co-located elements halves spares in Jones ICES-2015-044 worked example; no flight-quantified value
parameters.notes: Model as probability a corrective demand can be met from the failed-unit boneyard instead of stores; requires sub-ORU repair capability (tools, diagnostics, crew skill).

parameters.name: Fraction of MEL line items manufacturable on demand by near-term ISM (fluid-flow class)
parameters.source: Owens & de Weck, AIAA 2016-5394 (plumbing, ducting, fans, heat exchangers, tanks, valves ~33% of Master Equipment List; 50% used as growth sensitivity)
parameters.notes: Feasibility limited by production rate (AM: 0.01-10 kg/h) vs component time-to-effect (Moraguez & de Weck ICES-2020).

parameters.name: Maintenance-logistics mass reduction from ISM at fixed risk (33% item coverage)
parameters.source: Owens & de Weck, AIAA 2016-5394 (17,232 -> 12,323 kg at P(LoC contrib)=1e-3; 0.34 with feedstock recycling -> 11,254 kg; covered items alone: -78.3%, -97.7% with recycling)
parameters.notes: Also reduces sensitivity to MTBF uncertainty (median P(LoC) contrib 0.027 -> 0.0167 at 33%, 0.0084 at 50% coverage). Feedstock packaging overhead 0.015% vs 1.5% for spares.

parameters.name: Weibull shape parameter, pumps/fans/blowers/compressors (wear-out class)
parameters.source: Estimate: generic rotating-machinery reliability practice (Ebeling, Introduction to Reliability & Maintainability Engineering; OREDA-class industrial data); consistent with ISS pump wear-out history
parameters.notes: Scale eta set so distribution mean matches MTBF/K. ISS program itself assumes exponential (CFR) - use beta=1 at L0/L1, Weibull at L2.

parameters.name: Weibull shape parameter, electronics/controllers/sensors (random-failure class)
parameters.source: Chen, ICES-2025-127 (ISS program assumes constant failure rate / exponential, memoryless)
parameters.notes: Exponential inter-failure times; radiation environment on Mars transit may add an SEU-driven K-factor rather than shape change.

parameters.name: Weibull shape parameter, valves/actuators/mechanisms
parameters.source: Estimate: mixed random/wear behavior; CDRA check-valve dust-abrasion wear (Jones ICES-2019-14) supports beta>1 for particulate-exposed valves
parameters.notes: For Mars dust-exposed mechanisms (airlocks, EVA hardware) bias toward 1.5-2.0 and raise K during dust season (sensitivity parameter, no flight basis).

parameters.name: Weibull shape parameter, infant-mortality phase for newly installed/manufactured units
parameters.source: Estimate: bathtub-curve early phase (Ebeling; simplified bathtub in ICES-2025-127); 'first model year' effect discussed in Stromgren AIAA 2016-5307
parameters.notes: Apply for first ~500-1,000 operating hours of new-design or ISM-printed units, then transition to class shape.

parameters.name: Common-cause failure beta factor across identical redundant units/spares
parameters.source: Estimate: generic PRA beta-factor values (NUREG/CR-5485 class); documented ECLSS CCF chains (OGA cell stack, CDRA dust, UPA urine chemistry) per Jones ICES-2019-14
parameters.notes: Fraction of failures that defeat identical spares/redundancy. This is the term POS math ignores; dissimilar redundancy is the mitigation.

human_factors.title: Human Factors, Crew Time & Radiation
human_factors.modeling_notes: LABOR SUPPLY (per CM-sol, sol = 24.6597 h; ISS 24.0-h budgets scaled by 1.0275): Invariant time (sleep 8.5 + pre/post-sleep 1.5 + exercise 2.5) ~12.8 h/sol; on workdays add planning 2.0 and meals 3.0, leaving ~6.7-6.9 h/sol of assignable work inside the 6.5-h-equivalent ops block; weekly liens: housekeeping 4 h/CM-wk, food prep 0.17 (packaged) to 0.83+ (grown) h/CM-sol, ECLSS upkeep 3.0-3.3 h/habitat-day, corrective maintenance stochastic (mean 0.44, 99th pct 1.26 h/CM-sol, lognormal, scales with installed equipment). Schedule 5 workdays + 2 rest days per 7-sol week (rest days are a behavioral-health constraint; surge >VST max 76.7 h/wk sustainable <=28 d). EVA: cap 24 h/CM/wk with 1 full rest day; charge per-CM overhead (1.5 h design / up to ISS-actual 74 crew-h per EVA for sensitivity), O2 0.092 kg/CM-h, cooling water 0.25-0.76 kg/CM-h, suitport fixed 0.54 kg O2/CM-EVA; 30-sol post-landing reconditioning with no EVA. Cross-check: NASA's integrated Mars model yields only ~8.5% of gross surface crew-hours as science/utilization - if your sim gives >>15%, liens are being dropped. RADIATION TRACKER: L0 dose_mSv = 1.84*days_transit + 0.66*S*sols_surface, S = shielding transfer factor; track vs 600 mSv career and 250 mGy-Eq/30-day BFO. L1: modulate GCR +/-20% with 11-yr solar cycle (anti-correlated with sunspot number), atmospheric column correction around 23 g/cm2, S(depth) lookup MUST reproduce the dry-regolith neutron buildup hump (S rises to ~1.1-1.25 at ~90 g/cm2, crosses 1.0 at ~200 g/cm2, ~0.5 at ~350 g/cm2, <=0.3 beyond ~2 m); SPEs as Poisson events (lambda ~2-6/yr solar max, ~0.2-1/yr min) mostly negligible on surface behind atmosphere but requiring 12-36 h shelter occupancy (labor hit) and dominating transit acute risk. L2: BON2020/DLR GCR spectra + HZETRN/GEANT4 response matrices through time-varying column density (diurnal/seasonal pressure from MCD, ~5% sub-sol dose modulation), ICRP effective dose on phantoms, validated against RAD 0.64 mSv/d at Gale (Phi~580 MV). METRIC CONSISTENCY: RAD point dose-equivalent, Rostel water-sphere equivalent dose, and ICRP effective dose differ +/-30-50%; pick one (recommend effective dose) and convert sources consistently. MEDICAL: Poisson 3.4 minor + 0.017 evac-class events/person-yr with labor costs; no-evac reality means severe events convert to onboard care demand and possible crew loss (~0.01/mission floor).

parameters.name: Scheduled sleep per crewmember per day
parameters.source: NASA BVAD NASA/TP-2015-218570 Rev2 (2022), Table 3-28 (Langston 2005, ISS)
parameters.notes: Flight actuals: astronauts sleep only ~6.09 h/night on ISS (Barger et al. 2014, Lancet Neurol 13:904). Schedule 8.5 h; model the deficit as a performance risk, not recoverable labor. Scale by 1.0275 for per-sol budgets. An additional invariant 1.5 h/CM-day of pre-sleep (1.0) + post-sleep (0.5) time, including personal hygiene, brackets the sleep period (BVAD Table 3-28).

parameters.name: Scheduled work (systems+utilization+maintenance ops) per workday
parameters.source: NASA BVAD NASA/TP-2015-218570 Rev2 (2022), Table 3-28; ISS standard workweek
parameters.notes: 5 workdays + 2 weekend days/week (weekend has only ~0.3 h/d payload tending); 8 vacation days/yr. Mars surface work rate needs +0.7 h/CM-d vs ISS in NASA integrated model (Stromgren 2015).

parameters.name: Exercise incl. setup, stow, hygiene per day
parameters.source: NASA BVAD NASA/TP-2015-218570 Rev2 (2022), Table 3-28
parameters.notes: Device time ~1.5 h (aerobic+resistive), rest is overhead. BVAD assumes -0.5 h/d in hypogravity (surface); transit may require more. 7 days/week including rest days.

parameters.name: Meals (prep+consumption+cleanup) per day
parameters.source: NASA BVAD NASA/TP-2015-218570 Rev2 (2022), Table 3-28 (1.0 h per meal x 3)
parameters.notes: Prepackaged food system. Does not diminish on rest days. Preparation component broken out separately in hf.time.food_prep_* parameters.

parameters.name: Planning/reporting/work-prep overhead per workday
parameters.source: NASA BVAD NASA/TP-2015-218570 Rev2 (2022), Table 3-28 (DPC 0.5 + plan review/report 1.0 + work prep 0.5)
parameters.notes: Mars autonomy (4-24 min one-way light time) shifts this from ground-conference to onboard planning; NASA Mars crew time model increased training/work-prep liens for autonomous ops.

parameters.name: Housekeeping and laundry per week
parameters.source: NASA BVAD NASA/TP-2015-218570 Rev2 (2022), Table 3-28 (2.0 h on each of 2 weekend days)
parameters.notes: ISS actual scheduling. Larger habitats with dust ingress (Mars EVA ops) plausibly need more; upper range is an engineering allowance.

parameters.name: Recreation/off-duty block per weekend day (psychological constraint)
parameters.source: NASA BVAD NASA/TP-2015-218570 Rev2 (2022), Table 3-28
parameters.notes: Treat as a hard scheduling constraint (2 contiguous rest days/week) for behavioral health; includes private family/psych conferences ~1 h/wk. Not harvestable labor except in short (<28 d) surge periods.

parameters.name: Variably-Scheduled Time (taxable labor pool) per week, planetary surface
parameters.source: NASA BVAD NASA/TP-2015-218570 Rev2 (2022), Table 3-29 and sect. 3.2.5
parameters.notes: Includes work, meals, housekeeping, LSS ops. 67.2 h/wk in weightlessness (transit). Max (76.7) sustainable <=28 days per Skylab. Min 50 = minimum expected work output. Per sol multiply daily value by 1.0275.

parameters.name: Food preparation labor, fully prepackaged food system
parameters.source: NASA BVAD NASA/TP-2015-218570 Rev2 (2022), sect. 4.5.3 (Shuttle experience 45-90 min/d for crew of 6)
parameters.notes: Subset of the 3.0 h/d meals block. Use for resupplied-food scenarios.

parameters.name: Food preparation labor, fully crop-based (bioregenerative) diet
parameters.source: NASA BVAD NASA/TP-2015-218570 Rev2 (2022), sect. 4.5.3 (Hunter model; LMLSTP Phase III measured 4.6 CM-h/d for crew of 4)
parameters.notes: EXCLUDES crop processing into ingredients (unquantified in BVAD) and crop tending (belongs to food/plant domain). 50/50 mixed diet = 0.50 h/CM-day. Major labor lever for settlement-scale food closure.

parameters.name: ECLSS preventive+corrective upkeep per habitat per day (2-3 crew scale)
parameters.source: Russell & Klaus 2007, Reliab. Eng. Syst. Saf. 92:808-820 (ISS/Mir-derived)
parameters.notes: Per habitat, not per crewmember; scales sub-linearly with crew. Baseline for ISS-class ECLSS technology; regenerative closure increases it.

parameters.name: Corrective (unscheduled) maintenance, expected value
parameters.source: Stromgren et al. 2021, IEEE Aerospace, NTRS 20210026843 (lunar Surface Habitat, ISS component data: expected 24.7 h per 28-day 2-crew mission; 50th pct 9.0 h, 99th pct 70.5 h)
parameters.notes: Right-skewed (lognormal-like): range_min=50th pct, range_max=99th pct. ECLSS dominates. Scales with installed equipment count, not crew size; renormalize per habitat for settlement scale.

parameters.name: Maximum EVA time per crewmember per week
parameters.source: NASA HAT EVA team constraint, in Stromgren et al. 2015, NTRS 20150009469
parameters.notes: With one full rest day per 7 days. NASA model also imposes 30-day post-landing reconditioning before first EVA (long-stay). BVAD lunar outpost: 80 CM-h/wk total EVA at 7 two-person sorties/wk.

parameters.name: ISS EVA total prep + post crew-hours per EVA (actuals, microgravity EMU)
parameters.source: NASA EVA-EXP-0031 figures cited in Griffin, AIAA 2020-4170 (58.25 h prep + 15.83 h post, typically 2-person EVA)
parameters.notes: Total crew-hours across EV+IV crew over several days: procedures, tool config, suit servicing, prebreathe. Work efficiency index 0.39-0.51. Upper bound / legacy-ops anchor for Mars EVA overhead trades. ISS gas cost: ~9.1 kg prebreathe O2 + 1.8 kg airlock air lost per EVA.

parameters.name: Mars surface EVA overhead per crewmember per sortie (design case)
parameters.source: Estimate: exploration atmosphere 56.5 kPa/34% O2 gives ~15-min prebreathe (Abercromby et al. 2015, Acta Astronautica 109:76-87) + don/doff, checkout, dust mitigation, amortized suit servicing
parameters.notes: No flight data for planetary suits since Apollo; sensitivity-test between this and ISS actuals. Assumes suitport or high-cadence airlock ops.

parameters.name: EVA oxygen usage + leakage per suited hour
parameters.source: NASA BVAD NASA/TP-2015-218570 Rev2 (2022), Table 4-69 (lunar surface EVA values)
parameters.notes: xEMU suitport scenario: 0.10 kg/CM-h + 0.54 kg/CM-EVA fixed (N2 purge dominated). Metabolic rate suited: 295-352 W (Apollo surface avg ~1030 kJ/h).

parameters.name: EVA cooling water (sublimator/evaporator) loss per suited hour
parameters.source: NASA BVAD NASA/TP-2015-218570 Rev2 (2022), Table 4-69 (polar 0.25-0.5; equatorial 0.46-0.76)
parameters.notes: Thermal-environment dependent; lunar-derived. Mars ambient (colder, CO2 atmosphere) may allow radiator-augmented PLSS with lower water use; treat as upper-bound consumable.

parameters.name: Minimum acceptable Net Habitable Volume per crewmember, long-duration
parameters.source: Whitmire et al. 2015, NASA/TM-2015-218564 (SME consensus, NTRS 20140016951); consistent with NASA-STD-3001 Vol 2 task-based NHV requirement
parameters.notes: Floor for ~1-2.5 yr missions with countermeasures in place; permanent settlement plausibly needs more (no data; range_max is engineering allowance). Crew of 6 -> 150 m3 NHV minimum.

parameters.name: Private crew quarters volume per crewmember
parameters.source: Whitmire et al. 2015 consensus report (5.4 m3 allocation); ISS USOS CQ flight actual 2.1 m3 (Broyan et al., 08ICES-0222)
parameters.notes: 2.1 m3 (ISS) is the demonstrated floor and is considered marginal for multi-year missions; 5.4 m3 recommended for long-duration. Include radiation-augmented quarters if used as SPE shelter.

parameters.name: Mars surface GCR dose equivalent rate (Gale crater, solar max era)
parameters.source: Hassler et al. 2014, Science 343:1244797 (MSL RAD); solar-cycle range per Guo et al. 2021, Astron. Astrophys. Rev. 29:8
parameters.notes: +/-0.12 measurement uncertainty; = 0.66 mSv/sol = ~233 mSv/Earth-yr. Absorbed dose 0.21 mGy/d. Higher at solar min and at higher elevation (thinner atmosphere). Thin-detector point dose equivalent; ICRP effective dose ~30% lower (Martinez-Sierra 2024). verified correction: the 'Martinez-Sierra 2024' attribution for the ICRP-effective-dose reduction is a phantom author for arXiv:2409.02001 (actually Ralha et al. 2024).

parameters.name: Interplanetary transit GCR dose equivalent rate (light shielding, solar max)
parameters.source: Zeitlin et al. 2013, Science 340:1080-1084 (MSL RAD cruise, 2011-12)
parameters.notes: +/-0.33 uncertainty; measured at solar max behind ~10-40 g/cm2 nonuniform shielding. Solar-min GCR can be ~50-60% higher. Round-trip fast-transit (~360 d) GCR alone ~0.66 Sv. SPE contributions additional and stochastic.

parameters.name: NASA career effective dose limit
parameters.source: NASA-STD-3001 Vol 1 Rev B (2022), per 2021 NAS review; universal all ages/sexes, 3% mean REID basis
parameters.notes: Binding constraint: an unmitigated conjunction mission (~360 d transit + 500 sols surface) accrues ~1.0 Sv. Dose tracker should flag crew approaching limit and drive shielding/timing trades. A settlement sim may need its own (non-NASA) risk policy for permanent residents.

parameters.name: Short-term (30-day) blood-forming-organ dose limit
parameters.unit: mSv NASA effective dose per design-reference SPE (Oct 1989 spectrum)
parameters.source: NASA-STD-3001 Vol 1 Rev B [V1 4031], per OCHMO-TB-020 Rev E (12/2022); legacy 30-day BFO 250 mGy-Eq limit is NCRP-132/pre-Rev-B and Townsend et al. 2018
parameters.notes: Deterministic (acute) effects limit; the design driver for SPE storm shelters, separate from the 600 mSv career (cancer) limit. verified correction: NASA-STD-3001 Vol 1 Rev B (2022) does NOT contain a 30-day BFO 250 mGy-Eq limit; requirement [V1 4031] is <250 mSv NASA effective dose per design-reference SPE (Oct 1989 spectrum). The 30-day BFO 250 / 1-yr 500 mGy-Eq limits are the retired NCRP-132/pre-Rev-B values (still used in shelter-design literature). A rolling-30-day-BFO tracker would enforce a retired requirement.

parameters.name: Mean vertical atmospheric shielding column at datum
parameters.unit: g/cm2 (mean vertical column at low-elevation sites, Gale-like, -4.4 km MOLA; ~16 g/cm2 at MOLA datum)
parameters.source: Guo et al. 2021, Astron. Astrophys. Rev. 29:8; Ralha et al. 2024, arXiv:2409.02001
parameters.notes: Slant-path average shielding over the sky is substantially higher than vertical. Removes most SPE protons <150 MeV: SPE shelters are primarily a transit problem. Seasonal pressure cycle modulates surface dose by a few percent. verified correction: arXiv:2409.02001 is Ralha et al. 2024 (no author 'Martinez-Sierra'); 23 g/cm2 is the mean column at Gale/low-elevation sites (-4.4 km MOLA), not the datum. At the MOLA datum mean column is ~16 g/cm2 - relabel to avoid double-counting elevation scaling (~2%/(g/cm2) dose sensitivity).

parameters.name: Dry-regolith depth below which shielding is counterproductive (neutron buildup)
parameters.source: Röstel et al. 2020, JGR Planets 125:e2019JE006246 (GEANT4/AtRIS, RAD-validated): equivalent dose peaks at 30-40 cm (88-102 g/cm2) and only drops below surface value beyond ~200 g/cm2 (0.7-0.9 m dry rock)
parameters.notes: Model-critical: <0.7 m of dry regolith cover INCREASES crew equivalent dose (up to ~+10-25% at the ~30 cm peak). Buildup is much weaker for hydrogen-rich (icy) regolith. range_min = buildup peak location.

parameters.name: Dry regolith depth to roughly halve surface GCR equivalent dose
parameters.source: Röstel et al. 2020, Table 3: 1.26 m andesite (rho=2.8 g/cm3, ~350 g/cm2) reduces water-sphere equivalent dose to 200 mSv/yr, <=half the dry-surface value; sandstone 1.64 m
parameters.notes: In water-sphere equivalent-dose metric at medium solar modulation (Phi=580 MV). Loose regolith (rho~1.5-1.7) needs proportionally more thickness for equal areal density. Hydrated regolith halves dose in substantially less depth.

parameters.name: Regolith depth to bring annual equivalent dose under 100 mSv/yr
parameters.source: Röstel et al. 2020 (0.8-2.5 m depending on composition; hydrated less); Zhang et al. 2022 JGR Planets: 1-1.6 m at representative sites
parameters.notes: 100 mSv/yr threshold chosen as the level above which lifetime cancer risk increase is evident. Water-ice-rich regolith (10-50 wt%) is the most mass-efficient cover; 10% subsurface water content alone cuts above-ground equivalent dose ~36%.

parameters.name: Dedicated SPE storm-shelter shielding (transit; aluminum-equivalent)
parameters.source: Townsend et al. 2018, Life Sci. Space Res. 17:32-39 (PMID 29753411); Kim et al. 2006: 5 g/cm2 Al insufficient for BFO in Aug-1972-class event; ~10 g/cm2 meets 30-day BFO limits; 20+ g/cm2 (hydrogenous) for extreme events
parameters.notes: Water/polyethylene ~1.5-2x more effective than Al per g/cm2. Occupancy 12-36 h/event (all crew), warning minutes-hours: costs crew time and interrupts EVA. On surface, any >=200 g/cm2 regolith-covered module trivially satisfies SPE sheltering.

parameters.name: Clinical medical events (all severities), long-duration spaceflight
parameters.source: Crucian et al. 2016, Int. J. Gen. Med. 9:383-391 (ISS incidence; skin rash 1.12/py, upper-respiratory 0.97/py leading; infections ~0.6/py)
parameters.notes: Mostly minor/self-limited. Model as Poisson with per-category labor cost (patient downtime hours-days + caregiver ~0.5-2 h/d for significant events). ISS cohort is screened/healthy; settlement demographics would raise rates.

parameters.name: Evacuation-class medical events
parameters.source: Derived estimate from Antonsen et al. 2022 Fig. 4 (EVAC 0.3-1% per <42-d mission, 4 crew, unlimited ISS capability); not a stated IMM rate
parameters.notes: No actual ISS medical evacuation has occurred. For Mars (no evac possible): treat as severe-event rate requiring definitive onboard care; IMM projects >10% probability of an evac-class condition on a 730+ day mission even with ISS-level capability, and ~0.01 floor on medical loss-of-crew-life per 1195-d, 4-crew mission. verified correction: Antonsen et al. 2022 reports mission-level IMM probabilities (EVAC 0.3-1% for <42-day missions, >10% for 730+ day missions), not a 0.017 events/person-year rate; the value is derivable from those figures but should be labeled a derived estimate, not a published IMM rate.

robots.title: Robotic Labor
robots.modeling_notes: Core model: robot-hours/sol RH = N * A * H (availability A=0.85, productive hours H=12 h/sol per humanoid), converted to human-equivalent hours per task category via quality multipliers q_c: excavation = rate-based (42-174 kg/h per RASSOR/IPEx-class unit, 4 kWh/t digging + 2-3x system overhead), construction q=0.5, logistics q=0.6, inspection q=0.8, greenhouse tending q=0.25, complex repair q=0.15; multiply q by 1.5-3x EVA-leverage when robot-hours substitute crew EVA (EVA overhead was ~50% of the lunar-habitat maintenance crew-time estimate). Supervision is a hard constraint: Earth-Mars light time (3-22 min one-way) excludes direct teleoperation (collapses above ~0.7 s RTT), so pre-crew unstructured work must use rover-class multipliers (q~0.001, Squyres/Crawford), while local teleop from the hab restores warehouse-class ratios - model pre-crew and crewed phases with different q-vectors and a robots-per-supervisor limit (3, ramping to 10). Close two loops: (1) maintenance - robots consume 3 h tech labor per 100 robot-h, 10%/yr fleet mass in spares (26-month synod logistics: carry 25-30% fleet mass per window), fleet availability degrades when spares run out; (2) energy - 0.6 kWh per productive robot-hour (~9 kWh/sol/robot incl. night heating) charged against the power domain, with dust season reducing both available energy and robot availability simultaneously. ALL Optimus figures are company claims (dated 2022-2026, none independently verified; Musk 2026-01-28: fleet 'primarily for learning'); treat 57 kg / 2.3 kWh as design-class inputs and runtime/payload/speed claims as upper bounds. Recommended fidelity tiers: L0 scalar (10.2 robot-h/sol/robot * q_c), L1 sol-indexed with dust/power/aging/spares dynamics and autonomy learning curve, L2 sub-sol SOC-thermal-reliability simulation with task queues and teleop-slot scheduling (equations in robots.md).

parameters.name: Tesla Optimus mass (company claim)
parameters.source: Tesla Optimus Gen 2 unveil, 2023-12-12 (company claim, hardware shown); carried into Gen 3/V3 statements through 2026
parameters.notes: 125 lb / 173 cm. V3 mass not separately stated as of 2026-07; range allows growth for mass-production body. Company claim, not independently weighed.

parameters.name: Tesla Optimus battery capacity (company claim)
parameters.source: Tesla AI Day, 2022-09-30 (company claim): 2.3 kWh, 52 V pack
parameters.notes: V3 pack size unstated as of 2026-07; upper range allows a larger production pack. Usable energy will be ~80-90% of nameplate.

parameters.name: Optimus runtime per charge (claim vs realistic)
parameters.source: Tesla claim ('full work day' ~8 h, light tasks, AI Day 2022 + later); independent humanoid-deployment surveys show 2.5-4 h under sustained load
parameters.notes: 8 h consistent with 2.3 kWh only at ~290 W average (light duty). Use 3 h/charge for heavy Mars work in L1/L2 models. No independent 8 h demonstration exists as of 2026-07.

parameters.name: Optimus carry payload (company claim)
parameters.source: Tesla AI Day 2022 + subsequent statements: ~20 kg (45 lb) carry; ~68 kg (150 lb) deadlift claim repeated for Gen 3
parameters.notes: Company claim; light-object handling demonstrated, 68 kg deadlift never publicly shown. Mars gravity (0.379 g) roughly doubles-to-triples effective carriable mass at same joint torque, minus dynamic stability limits.

parameters.name: Optimus walking speed (demonstrated / claimed)
parameters.source: Gen 2 video 2023-12-12 (~0.6 m/s, '30% faster'); V3 claim 5 mph = 2.2 m/s (Musk statements 2025-2026, company claim)
parameters.notes: Use 0.5-0.8 m/s for Mars planning (unstructured terrain, conservative gait); 2.2 m/s is an undemonstrated claim. Compare flight rovers: 0.01-0.05 m/s.

parameters.name: Optimus hand degrees of freedom (company claim)
parameters.source: Gen 3 hand claim: 22 DoF hand/forearm, tactile fingertips (We,Robot 2024-10-10 and later Musk statements); Gen 2 demonstrated 11 DoF (2023-12-12)
parameters.notes: 22-DoF hand demos to date were teleoperated; autonomous dexterity unproven. Human hand ~27 DoF. Hand is the reliability weak point for dusty Mars work (no dust-sealing data published).

parameters.name: Optimus idle power draw (company claim)
parameters.source: Tesla AI Day, 2022-09-30 (company claim): ~100 W sitting
parameters.notes: Upper range covers compute-on standby drain observed in deployed humanoids (15-20% of pack per 8 h idle). Mars cold adds heater load on top - see robots.power.energy_per_work_hour.

parameters.name: Optimus working power draw (claim, with space-humanoid upper bound)
parameters.source: Tesla AI Day 2022 claim: ~500 W brisk walking; upper bound = NASA Valkyrie measured class (1.8 kWh / ~1 h runtime = ~1.8 kW)
parameters.notes: Heavy manipulation/carry likely 600-900 W on Optimus-class actuators. Use 500 W nominal, 1.8 kW conservative for space-hardened variants.

parameters.name: Earth-Mars one-way light time
parameters.source: Orbital geometry; ESA Mars Express blog (2012); NASA ASCEND crewed-Mars comm-delay paper NTRS 20220013418
parameters.notes: Round trip 6-45 min plus DSN scheduling and relay store-and-forward; plus ~2-week conjunction blackout each synod. Rules out Earth-based direct teleoperation of manipulation entirely.

parameters.name: Max round-trip latency for direct manipulation teleoperation
parameters.source: Telerobotics literature: degradation onset ~300 ms RTT, near-collapse of direct control by ~0.7-1 s (Frontiers Robotics & AI 2020 survey; vision-teleop latency studies)
parameters.notes: Above this, operators must use supervisory/scripted control with large productivity loss. Local teleop from hab (RTT < 50 ms) is unconstrained; Earth teleop (RTT >= 360 s) is excluded. This binary is a structural feature of the model.

parameters.name: Productive robot work hours per sol (humanoid, settlement ops)
parameters.source: Estimate: battery bouts 2.5-4 h + 1-3 h charge cycles (Agility Digit 2:1 work:charge company claim, 2024-2025), night cold/power limits, tasking gaps
parameters.notes: Duty cycle ~0.5 of the 24.66 h sol. Upper bound 18 h requires battery swap + nuclear power + night ops. Multiply by fleet availability (robots.maint.fleet_availability) for net robot-hours.

parameters.name: Humanoid recharge time per battery cycle
parameters.source: Estimate: 2.3 kWh pack at ~1C charging with thermal management margin; no Tesla statement found as of 2026-07
parameters.notes: Battery swap (unannounced for Optimus) would reduce effective charge time to minutes at the cost of spare packs (~10-15 kg each) - model as a design option.

parameters.name: Robots supervised per human (local teleop/exception handling, early ops)
parameters.source: Estimate: warehouse AMR/humanoid pilot staffing analogies; Optimus factory ops 2025-2026 still teleop-heavy (Tesla Q4-2025 earnings call, 2026-01-28)
parameters.notes: Ramp toward 10:1 over 2-3 synods as autonomy matures. Binding constraint on unstructured work: teleop slots, not robot count. Pre-crew phase: supervision only from Earth at sol-scale - apply q_rover_science_legacy to unstructured tasks.

parameters.name: Fraction of structured-task robot-hours needing no human in the loop
parameters.source: Estimate: warehouse humanoid deployments run largely autonomous with exception intervention (Agility/GXO 100k-tote milestone, company release 2025-11); Optimus autonomy 'still R&D' per Musk 2026-01-28
parameters.notes: For unstructured repair/dexterous tasks assume <= 0.2 autonomous. Intervention events well-modeled as Poisson with ~15 min median handling time.

parameters.name: Quality multiplier: humanoid vs human, structured tasks (construction/logistics)
parameters.source: Agility Digit @ GXO implied 60-75% of human picker throughput (press analyses of company ROI claims, 2024-2025); Amazon purpose-built stow robot measured 224 vs human 243 units/h (arXiv:2505.04572, 2025)
parameters.notes: Best-evidenced ratio class in the domain. Applies to robot-adapted, repetitive tasks with local supervision available. Add EVA leverage x1.5-3 when the robot-hour substitutes crew EVA time.

parameters.name: Quality multiplier: humanoid vs human, unstructured field/repair tasks
parameters.source: Estimate: no deployed evidence exists; bounded above by structured-task ratios and below by rover field experience; consistent with 2026 state of humanoid autonomy (teleop-dependent for novel manipulation)
parameters.notes: Assumes local teleop available (hab). Without it (pre-crew), use robots.work.q_rover_science_legacy for these tasks. Largest single sensitivity in the domain: settlement-level robot value swings ~5x across this range.

parameters.name: Quality multiplier: sol-scale supervised rover vs human field scientist (flight experience)
parameters.source: Squyres (MER PI) 2005, repeated 2022: what a rover does in a perfect sol, a human field geologist does in ~30-45 s; Crawford, Astron. & Geophys. 2012 (arXiv:1203.6250)
parameters.notes: Computed as ~45 s human / (9 h rover active sol). Documented expert estimate, not a measurement. Governs pre-crew unstructured work under Earth-only supervision; the 350x gap to q_structured quantifies the value of local supervision + structured tasking.

parameters.name: Specialized excavator robot regolith excavation rate (RASSOR/IPEx class)
parameters.source: IPEx mission spec: 10,000 kg at 42 kg/h over 11-day lunar demo (Schuler et al., ASCEND 2024, NASA KSC); RASSOR 2.0 implied continuous rate 66 kg / 0.38 = ~174 kg/h (NTRS 20210011366)
parameters.notes: TRL-5 design values, not flight data. Per ~30-66 kg robot. Lunar-designed; Mars gravity (2.3x lunar) improves traction/dig force, duricrust may resist. Scale linearly with robot mass at ~0.38 kg vehicle mass per kg/h (RASSOR 2.0 figure of merit).

parameters.name: Specific digging energy, bucket-drum excavation (excl. drive/avionics/thermal)
parameters.source: RASSOR 2.0 paper: 4 W per (kg/h) excavation rate = 4 Wh/kg digging-only (NTRS 20210011366); upper range = x2-3 total-system overhead estimate
parameters.notes: Use 4 kWh/t for the digging subsystem (medium confidence, documented), 8-12 kWh/t delivered-to-hopper total system (low). Cross-check: RASSOR 1410 Wh battery + 2.7 t/day implies multiple charge cycles/day - consistent.

parameters.name: Specialized construction robot speedup vs human (single structured task)
parameters.source: Terrestrial analogy, company claims: FBR Hadrian X 200-300 blocks/h (2023 test records) vs human mason 300-500 bricks/day
parameters.notes: Applies only to tasks re-engineered around the robot (block laying, panel placement, regolith 3D printing) - not to humanoids doing human-style construction. Argues the sim should let users buy specialized machines per task category rather than only general humanoids.

parameters.name: Humanoid MTBF, Mars field environment (failures requiring intervention)
parameters.source: Estimate: bounded by FANUC factory-arm vendor claim (~80,000 h MTBF, benign environment) above and IPEx TRL-5 dusty wheel-actuator life test (~1x 11-day mission, bearing failure mode found; Schuler ASCEND 2024) below
parameters.notes: 50-actuator humanoid in abrasive dust and -80C nights will sit far below factory-arm MTBF; practitioner data shows ~88% availability even for terrestrial cells. Least-supported number in the domain - carry the full 100-2000 h range in trade studies. Weibull shape 1.5-2 recommended for L1/L2.

parameters.name: Maintenance labor per 100 robot operating hours
parameters.source: Estimate: MTBF 500 h x MTTR ~4-8 h + preventive (dust cleaning, lube, inspection) ~1-2 h/100 h; consistent with industrial-robot PM schedules (~every 3,000-10,000 h) heavily derated for dust
parameters.notes: At 12 h/sol each robot consumes ~0.36 h/sol technician time; a 10-robot fleet ~= 0.55 crew-equivalents if humans do all of it. Robots self-maintaining at q~0.15 closes part of the loop; verify convergence N*m*(1-r_self*q) < crew budget.

parameters.name: Robot fleet spares mass fraction per Earth-year
parameters.source: Anchored on ISS supportability: ~10 t/yr spares upmass (NTRS 20150003005); Owens & de Weck Mars-One analysis (MIT 2014): spares -> 64% of resupply mass by year 10
parameters.notes: Per 26-month synod carry >= 2.2 yr + reserve: 25-30% of fleet mass per window. Cannibalization of failed units recovers ~50% of spares demand at cost of fleet size. High-wear items: hands, hip/knee actuators, batteries (batteries ~500-1000 cycles = 1-2 Mars years at 1 cycle/sol).

parameters.name: Robot fleet availability (fraction not down for maintenance/repair)
parameters.source: Derived: A = MTBF/(MTBF + MTTR_effective) with MTTR_eff including diagnosis, spares wait, repair queue; consistent with terrestrial robot-cell availability ~88% (The Robot Report)
parameters.notes: Degrade toward 0.7 when spares inventory empties late in a synod or during dust season (solar power constrained + dust-driven failure rate up).

parameters.name: Electrical energy per productive humanoid robot-work-hour (incl. charging loss + Mars thermal)
parameters.source: Derived: 400-500 W claimed working draw (Tesla AI Day 2022) / 0.9 charge efficiency + 50-150 W thermal overhead; upper bound Valkyrie-class measured ~1.8 kW (NASA R5 fact sheet)
parameters.notes: ~7 kWh/sol/robot at 12 h/sol + ~1-3 kWh/night survival heating -> ~9 kWh/sol/robot budget. 100-robot fleet ~ 0.9 MWh/sol ~ 25-40 kW continuous - first-order load for the power domain.

parameters.name: ANCHOR: RASSOR 2.0 excavation throughput
parameters.source: NASA KSC RASSOR 2.0 (NTRS 20210011366): 66 kg robot, 'minimum 2.7 metric tons per day'; 4.2 t/day implied continuous at 174 kg/h
parameters.notes: TRL-4 breadboard, Earth testing, lunar design case. Headline 2.7 t/day embeds ~0.65 duty cycle. One RASSOR-class unit ~= one suited human shoveling full-time, with zero EVA cost.

parameters.name: ANCHOR: ATHLETE heavy cargo robot payload (Earth g prototype)
parameters.source: JPL Robotics ATHLETE program: Gen 1 850 kg vehicle / 300 kg payload; Gen 2 (Tri-ATHLETE) 450 kg payload at Earth g, ~10 km/h max speed
parameters.notes: High confidence as prototype spec (never flown). At Mars 0.379 g the same limbs handle ~2.6x mass -> ~1.2 t class. Template for the 'heavy logistics/cargo' robot category (Starship unloading, module relocation).

parameters.name: ANCHOR: Curiosity average drive distance per driving sol (flight data)
parameters.source: Rankin et al., 'Driving Curiosity: Mars Rover Mobility Trends During the First Seven Years', IEEE Aerospace 2020, NTRS 20220000780; lifetime 36.9 km / 4942 sols = 7.5 m/sol all-sol average (JPL, as of mid-2026)
parameters.notes: MER anchors: top speed 0.05 m/s, ~0.01 m/s under autonomous hazard avoidance; Opportunity lifetime 45.16 km/5111 sols = 8.8 m/sol, record 220 m/sol. These flight rates embody sol-scale Earth supervision + extreme risk aversion - the floor of the productivity scale.

parameters.name: ANCHOR: NASA Valkyrie space-design humanoid average power draw
parameters.source: NASA JSC R5/Valkyrie fact sheet; Radford et al., J. Field Robotics 2015: 125 kg, 44 DoF, 1.8 kWh battery, ~1 h runtime
parameters.notes: The only NASA-built humanoid power datum. 3-4x the Tesla claim - use as the conservative bound for space-hardened humanoid energy modeling.

parameters.name: ANCHOR: Expected corrective maintenance crew time, small surface habitat
parameters.source: Stromgren et al., 'Assessment of Crew Time for Maintenance and Repair Activities for Lunar Surface Missions', IEEE Aerospace 2021, NTRS 20210026843, Table 3 (expected value; 50th pct 9.0 h, 99th pct 70.5 h)
parameters.notes: ISS-data-derived, 2-crew 28-day lunar surface habitat. EVA overhead is 12.52 of the 24.7 h - the component robots most credibly replace. Sizes the 'inspection/maintenance' task-category demand that robot-hours bid against; scale with habitat complexity.

mission_architecture.title: Mission Architecture & Trajectories
mission_architecture.modeling_notes: CAMPAIGN CLOCK: model launch opportunities on the 779.94-d synodic period using the per-window table from NASA/TM-2010-216764 (departure dates walk Oct 2026 -> Dec 2028 -> Feb 2031 -> Apr 2033 -> Apr 2035 -> Jun 2037 -> Jul 2039; min C3 varies 7.78-14.84 km2/s2, cheapest 2033, dearest 2037-39, so payload per fixed vehicle varies ~2x across the cycle). Convert C3 to TMI dv via dv = sqrt(C3 + 2*mu_E/r) - v_circ; Mars entry velocity via v_e = sqrt(vinf^2 + 5.03^2) km/s. ARCHITECTURE: conjunction-class only for baseline (transits 150-210 d NASA / 90-150 d Starship-fast; stay ~500-620 sols set by return-window phasing t_stay ~ S - TOF_out - TOF_back; total ~900-950 d); opposition-class (30-90 d stay, 500-650 d total, Venus swing-by, much higher energy) only as variant. Missing the Mars-departure return window costs +759 sols - the contingency sizing case for surface consumables/power. LOGISTICS: each Mars ship needs 10-16 LEO tanker flights completed inside a ~30-d launch period, so Earth pad cadence x depot boil-off caps ships/window; returning ships need ~1100-1500 t ISRU propellant produced before the return window opens. EDL: commit-to-land, no abort-to-orbit (DRA 5.0 finding); direct entry 5.7-9.7 km/s depending on transit speed (deceleration burn option); use DRA 5.0 Table 4-3 (109.7 t entry -> 56.8 t landed, beta = 471 kg/m2) as the validated anchor and treat Starship 100 t landed as low-confidence company claim (DLR 2024 disputes closure). SITES: constrain to Arcadia/Amazonis band (lat <40N, elev <= -2 km, slopes <5 deg, shallow SWIM ice). COMMS: OWLT 3-22 min; hard 12-d blackout each synodic cycle (SEP <2 deg). ABORT STATE MACHINE: pre-TMI full abort; post-TMI 2-yr free-return (costly, high reentry speeds); Mars-orbit safe-haven loiter to TEI window; EDL no abort; surface shelter-in-place, ascent only when MAV/ship fueled (verify-before-crew-departs gate per DRA 5.0). All SpaceX dates/fleet sizes are company claims as of mid-2026: first uncrewed attempt slipped (Feb 9, 2026 announcement) from the Nov-Dec 2026 window to 2028/29; crew no earlier than ~2031.

parameters.name: Earth-Mars synodic period
parameters.source: Computed from NASA Mars Fact Sheet orbital periods (365.256 d / 686.980 d); standard value used throughout NASA/TM-2010-216764
parameters.notes: Launch windows recur every ~25.6 months (= 759.1 sols). Window-to-window spacing varies slightly with Mars orbital eccentricity.

parameters.name: Mean Mars solar day (sol)
parameters.source: Allison & McEwen (2000), Planet. Space Sci. 48; NASA GISS Mars24
parameters.notes: = 24.6597 h. 1 Mars year = 686.98 d = 668.6 sols. Conversion: days x 0.97324 = sols.

parameters.name: Minimum departure C3, 2026 window
parameters.source: NASA/TM-2010-216764 Tables 1-2 (Burke, Falck, McGuire 2010)
parameters.notes: Type II optimum: depart 2026-10-31, arrive 2027-08-19 (292 d), arrival Vinf 2.73 km/s. Type I: C3 11.11, 2026-11-14 -> 2027-08-09 (268 d). This was SpaceX's originally targeted first uncrewed window (Nov-Dec 2026), abandoned Feb 2026.

parameters.name: Minimum departure C3, 2028/29 window
parameters.source: NASA/TM-2010-216764 Tables 1,3
parameters.notes: Type II: depart 2028-12-02, arrive 2029-10-16 (318 d). Type I: C3 9.05, 2028-12-10 -> 2029-07-20 (222 d). Current SpaceX target window for first uncrewed Starships after Feb 2026 delay.

parameters.name: Minimum departure C3, 2031 window
parameters.source: NASA/TM-2010-216764 Tables 1,4

parameters.name: Minimum departure C3, 2033 window
parameters.source: NASA/TM-2010-216764 Tables 1,5
parameters.notes: Cheapest window of the 2026-2045 span. Type II: depart 2033-04-28, arrive 2034-01-27 (274 d). Type I: C3 8.41, 2033-04-06 -> 2033-10-01 (178 d). Kingdon (2025) 90-day crew trajectory departs 2033-04-30.

parameters.name: Minimum departure C3, 2035 window
parameters.source: NASA/TM-2010-216764 Tables 1,6
parameters.notes: Anomalous window: Type I is optimal (depart 2035-04-21, arrive 2035-11-03, 196 d) because Type II arrival coincides with Mars aphelion (Type II C3 17.52).

parameters.name: Minimum departure C3, 2037 window
parameters.source: NASA/TM-2010-216764 Tables 1,7
parameters.notes: Most expensive window of the cycle (with 2039: C3 12.17, Type II 2039-07-15 -> 2040-07-09). Type II: 2037-06-18 -> 2038-07-19 (396 d); Type I: C3 17.07, 198 d. Payload per fixed vehicle drops ~2x vs 2033. 2041/2043/2045 minima: 9.82/8.97/8.59.

parameters.name: Practical launch period per window
parameters.source: Estimate: C3 penalty of a few km2/s2 buys ~+/-2-3 weeks around optimum (TM-2010-216764 contours); robotic missions hold ~3-week periods; SpaceX stated 'Nov-Dec 2026' (~45 d)
parameters.notes: All ships of a wave plus their 10-16 tanker flights each must complete inside this period -> Earth pad cadence is the campaign bottleneck.

parameters.name: Cargo transit time (min-energy ballistic)
parameters.source: NASA/TM-2010-216764 (Type I/II min-energy TOFs 178-412 d across 2026-2039); DRA 5.0 cargo legs 202-350 d
parameters.notes: Value = typical Type II. Kingdon (2025) assumes slow cargo Starships needing only 4 LEO refuels. Cargo pre-deploys one full window (~26 mo) ahead of crew in NASA DRA 5.0.

parameters.name: Crew transit time, NASA reference (fast conjunction)
parameters.source: NASA-SP-2009-566 (DRA 5.0): 174 d outbound / 201 d inbound for 2037 NTR all-propulsive case; class characterized as 'less than 180 to 210 days'
parameters.notes: Transits minimized to limit crew GCR/SPE exposure and zero-g deconditioning.

parameters.name: Crew transit time, SpaceX claim
parameters.source: Company claim: Musk IAC 2016/2017 (New Space 2017/2018); '80-150 days depending on window, average ~115 d'
parameters.notes: Claim is well documented; achievability depends on refilled Starship delta-v (~4-4.6 km/s) and unproven high-speed Mars entry (7.5+ km/s).

parameters.name: Fast crew transit (peer-reviewed Starship trajectory)
parameters.source: Kingdon, Scientific Reports 15:17764 (2025), doi:10.1038/s41598-025-00565-7
parameters.notes: Round trips: depart 2033-04-30 (90 d out, 90 d back, Earth return 2035-07-02) and 2035-07-15 (104 d). C3 ~23-32 km2/s2; ~3.5-4.6 km/s post-refill delta-v incl 0.5 km/s landing burn; 15 LEO refuels per crew ship; pre-entry deceleration burn cuts Mars arrival 9.73 -> 6.87 km/s; peak entry load 3.5 g. Keeps mission dose within NASA career radiation limits where 180-d transits do not.

parameters.name: Surface stay, conjunction-class mission
parameters.source: NASA-SP-2009-566 (DRA 5.0): 539 days = 525 sols; class defined as '500 days or more'
parameters.notes: Stay is set by Earth-return window phasing (t_stay ~ synodic - TOF_out - TOF_back), not logistics. Fast 90-104 d transits stretch stay to ~595 sols (Kingdon). Missing the return window costs +759 sols (one synodic period) - key contingency sizing case.

parameters.name: Total round-trip duration, conjunction-class
parameters.source: NASA-SP-2009-566 (DRA 5.0), 2037 reference mission: 174 + 539 + 201 = 914 d; class ~900-950 d
parameters.notes: Upper range covers fast-transit variants with longer stays and window slack.

parameters.name: Surface stay, opposition-class (short-stay) mission
parameters.source: NASA-SP-2009-566 (DRA 5.0) section 6.2: 'short surface stay times at Mars (typically 30 to 90 days)', total mission 500-650 d
parameters.notes: Requires Venus swing-by or deep-space maneuver, perihelion pass <=0.7 AU, much higher total energy. Rejected by DRA 5.0 and incompatible with ISRU-refill Starship return; include in sim only as a high-energy abort/architecture variant.

parameters.name: LEO tanker refueling flights per Mars-bound Starship
parameters.source: Company claim + NASA statements: ~1200 t propellant load at ~100-150 t/tanker; NASA HLS 'high teens' (L. Hawkins, Nov 2023); Aerospace America (2025) analysis: 12/ship, 60 launches for the 5-ship wave; Kingdon 2025: 15 (crew)/4 (cargo)
parameters.notes: Strong function of tanker variant payload (100 vs 150-200 t) and boil-off; Musk has claimed as few as 4-8. Refills must complete within the ~30-60 d launch period.

parameters.name: Starship payload landed on Mars surface
parameters.source: Company claim (Musk IAC 2017, New Space 2018; SpaceX statements 2016-2025: '100 t class, up to 150 t with full refill')
parameters.notes: Disputed: Maiwald et al. (Sci Rep 2024) could not reproduce the mass model and find the return leg infeasible without near-full ISRU reload. Sim should include a downside toggle to ~50 t.

parameters.name: ISRU propellant required per returning Starship
parameters.source: Derived from SpaceX propellant capacity claims (933 t LOX + 267 t LCH4; V3 ~1500 t) and Maiwald et al. 2024 minimum Mars-surface-to-Earth return delta-v of 7.12 km/s
parameters.notes: LOX:LCH4 ~ 3.5:1. Scale gap: MOXIE produced ~122 g O2 total (2021-2023) vs ~600-900 t O2 needed per ship; power O(0.6-1+ MWe) continuous. Lower bound ~600 t if direct-return with partial load proves possible.

parameters.name: Mars atmospheric entry velocity (crewed Starship)
parameters.source: Company claim: Musk IAC 2017 ('entering very quickly, going seven and a half kilometers a second'); cross-checked v_entry = sqrt(vinf^2 + 5.03^2)
parameters.notes: Min-energy arrivals: 5.7-6.2 km/s (MSL was 5.8). 90-d transits: 8.5-9.7 km/s unless a pre-entry deceleration burn is made (Kingdon: burn to 6.87 km/s, peak 3.5 g). Starship TPS entry-corridor limit at Mars is not publicly established. Direct entry from interplanetary (no aerocapture-to-orbit), lifting entry L/D ~0.3, terminal propulsive burn ~0.5 km/s.

parameters.name: Starship Mars landing accuracy (3-sigma radius)
parameters.source: Estimate: extrapolation from Starship meter-class propulsive precision on Earth (tower catches 2024-25) and Mars state of the art (Perseverance TRN, ~1-2 km from ellipse center in 7.7x6.6 km ellipse); no SpaceX figure published
parameters.notes: Base build-out (landing near pre-placed depots/habs) requires <1 km spacing. DRA 5.0 abort-to-surface philosophy also requires landing within crew-rover reach of pre-deployed assets.

parameters.name: Landing site latitude criterion
parameters.source: Golombek et al., 'SpaceX Starship Landing Sites on Mars', 52nd LPSC #2420 (2021): latitude <40 deg for solar power and thermal management
parameters.notes: Tension: shallow ice becomes more abundant poleward; Arcadia Planitia (~38-44N) is the sweet spot with ice consistency ~0.6-0.7 at ~40N. Seven candidate sites: AP-1/8/9 (Arcadia), EM-15/16 (Erebus Montes), PM-1/7 (Phlegra Montes); AP-8 rates best on combined ice+safety (SWIM) scores.

parameters.name: Landing site elevation criterion
parameters.source: Golombek et al., 52nd LPSC #2420 (2021): below -2 km required, <= -3 km preferred, for EDL deceleration timeline of heavy landers
parameters.notes: Additional criteria: slopes <5 deg over 10 m; probability of rocks >0.5 m below 5%; radar-reflective load-bearing surface; no dense expanded secondary craters.

parameters.name: Solar conjunction communication blackout
parameters.source: NASA NTRS 20220013418 (ASCEND 2022): Sun-Earth-Mars angle <2 deg criterion gives ~11-14 d (mean 11.8 d); <3 deg up to ~3 weeks; <1 deg under a week
parameters.notes: Occurs once per synodic period (~26 months); flight practice (e.g., Oct 2-16, 2021 moratorium) ~2 weeks. One-way light time 3-22 min (6-44 min two-way) -> EDL, medical, ISRU fault handling must be autonomous.

parameters.name: DRA 5.0 landed useful mass per lander
parameters.source: NASA-SP-2009-566 Table 4-3 (EDL System Characteristics)
parameters.notes: Entry mass 109.7 t, 10x30 m triconic aeroshell, ballistic coefficient 471 kg/m2, descent dv 595 m/s, ignition 1350 m / Mach 2.29, peak heating 131 W/cm2. Landed/entry ratio ~0.52. Two such landers (MAV+ISRU+reactor; SHAB) pre-deployed per crew-of-6 mission, one window ahead; MAV verified fully fueled before crew departs Earth.

parameters.name: DRA 5.0 total IMLEO per crewed mission
parameters.source: NASA-SP-2009-566 section 4: NTR option 848.7 t (9 Ares V launches); chemical/aerocapture option 1251.8 t (12 Ares V)
parameters.notes: Per mission = 2 cargo vehicles (246.2 t IMLEO each, delivering 103 t payload elements) + crew MTV (356.4 t). Useful cross-check: ~850-1250 t IMLEO buys ~110-120 t landed + crew round trip for 6.

parameters.name: DRA 5.0 surface power (fission reactor)
parameters.source: NASA-SP-2009-566 section 5.4: 30-kWe version of 40-kWe lunar fission design; deployed 1 km from lander, 30-40 sols to emplace
parameters.notes: Load breakdown: ISRU O2 production 25 kWe continuous (dominant), habitat 12 kWe day/night. ISRU makes ascent-stage LOX only (CH4 from Earth; 400 kg H2 seed for water). SpaceX-scale full-propellant ISRU needs ~20-40x this power.

parameters.name: Mars Direct TMI throw mass per launch
parameters.source: Zubrin & Baker (1991), Mars Direct; Ares HLV: 47 t trans-Mars injection (121 t to 300 km LEO); via Encyclopedia Astronautica
parameters.notes: 2 launches per crew-of-4 mission, offset one window: ERV ~28.5 t landed (makes CH4/O2 from 6 t H2 seed) + Hab ~25 t landed. Per-crew landed mass ~13 t/person vs DRA 5.0 ~19-28 t/person - useful lower/upper band for settlement cargo manifests.

parameters.name: SpaceX ships-per-window ambition (2033 window)
parameters.source: Company claim (Musk, Starbase talk, May 29, 2025): 5 ships 2026/27 -> ~20 in 2028/29 -> ~100 in 2030/31 -> ~500 by 2033; long-term 1000-2000/window
parameters.notes: Treat as upper-bound scenario input, not manifest. Feb 9, 2026 delay announcement shifts the whole ramp right by >=1 synodic cycle. Each ship also implies 10-16 tanker launches: 500 ships -> ~6000+ Earth launches per window.

parameters.name: First uncrewed Starship Mars attempt (company target)
parameters.source: Company claim: Sep 7, 2024 target of 5 ships at Nov-Dec 2026 window was abandoned Feb 9, 2026 (Musk: Mars delayed 'about five to seven years' to focus on lunar missions); first attempt now the 2028/29 window
parameters.notes: Status as of mid-2026. Mission profile per 2024-25 statements: ~5 uncrewed ships carrying Tesla Optimus robots to test landing intact, survey ice, begin infrastructure.

parameters.name: First crewed Mars landing (company target)
parameters.source: Company claims: Musk Mar 15, 2025 ('as soon as 2029, although 2031 is more likely'); Feb 9, 2026 delay statement pushes credible first crew to ~2031-2033
parameters.notes: Every SpaceX crewed-Mars date since 2016 has slipped ~1 synodic cycle per cycle elapsed. NASA M2M planning holds crewed Mars in the late 2030s-2040s. Sim should treat crew date as a scenario variable gated on uncrewed landing success + ISRU demo.

mars_environment.title: Mars Surface Environment
mars_environment.modeling_notes: TIME/SUN CORE: t[sols] -> Ls via Allison & McEwen 2000 series (M = 19.3871 + 0.52402073*dtJ2000; equation of center; Ls = alphaFMS + (nu-M); EOT = 2.861 sin2Ls - 0.071 sin4Ls + 0.002 sin6Ls - (nu-M)). Declination: delta = arcsin(0.42565 sin Ls) + 0.25deg sin Ls. Hour angle h = 15deg*(LTST-12); cos z = sin phi sin delta + cos phi cos delta cos h; sunrise/sunset: cos h_ss = -tan phi tan delta; daylength = (h_ss/180deg)*24.6597 h. TOA flux: S = 586.2*[(1+e cos(Ls-251deg))/(1-e^2)]^2 W/m^2. Surface: direct = S exp(-tau/cos z); total horizontal = S cos z * f(tau,z) with Appelbaum & Flood 1990 net-flux table (f(0deg) ~ 0.88/0.72/0.50/0.33/0.17 at tau 0.4/1/2/3/5 - diffuse keeps arrays alive in storms). PRESSURE: 2-harmonic annual fit through Gale landmarks (Ls,Pa): (0,860),(55,890),(150,730),(255,950); scale exp(-dz/11.1km) for site elevation; rho = p*0.04334/(8.314*T). TEMPERATURE: L1 = seasonal sinusoids for daily Tmax/Tmin anchored to REMS values with asymmetric intra-sol shape; L2 = 1D conduction PDE (15-node, 1 m grid, k=0.039, rho*c=1.0e6) with surface balance (1-A)G + eps*sigma(Tsky^4-Ts^4) + h(Tair-Ts), h = 0.4-4.0 W/m^2K. DUST: tau(Ls) climatology (0.4 clear season, 0.9 dusty season) + Poisson regional storms (~3/MY in Ls 135-330, +tau 2-4, 15-40 sols) + Bernoulli GDS (p=0.33/MY, onset Ls 185-310, peak tau 5-11, 60-150 sols). Deposition 0.2%/sol on arrays (no scheduled cleaning credit). Sky temp for radiators: 140-160 K night, ~185 K day (tau=1), -> air temp (210-230 K) in GDS. UNIT TRAPS: visible-extinction tau = ~2.6x Montabone 9.3um CDOD; solar constant convention 1361 vs 1371 W/m^2; '600-750 Pa' folklore is datum elevation - real (low) sites see 700-1000 Pa.

parameters.name: Sol (mean Mars solar day) length
parameters.source: Allison & McEwen (2000), Planet. Space Sci. 48; NASA GISS Mars24 algorithm
parameters.notes: = 24.6597 h = 1.0274912517 Earth days. Canonical sim time unit.

parameters.name: Mars year length
parameters.source: Allison & McEwen (2000)
parameters.notes: = 686.97 Earth days. Season lengths (sols): Ls 0-90: 193.3; 90-180: 178.6; 180-270: 141.9; 270-360: 154.4 (Kepler asymmetry). verified correction: Kepler-exact season lengths (e=0.0934, Ls_peri=251, T=668.5991 sols) are Ls 0-90: 193.3; 90-180: 178.6; 180-270: 142.7; 270-360: 154.0 (sum 668.6); the prior 141.9/154.4 were slightly off and summed to 668.2.

parameters.name: Surface gravitational acceleration
parameters.source: NSSDC Mars Fact Sheet (NASA GSFC)
parameters.notes: 0.379 g. Range reflects latitude/elevation variation.

parameters.name: Axial obliquity (current epoch)
parameters.source: NSSDC Mars Fact Sheet; Allison & McEwen (2000) use sin(25.19 deg)=0.42565 in declination formula
parameters.notes: Solar declination: delta = arcsin(0.42565 sin Ls) + 0.25 deg * sin Ls.

parameters.name: Orbital eccentricity
parameters.source: NSSDC Mars Fact Sheet
parameters.notes: Drives +/-19% swing in TOA solar flux over the year (vs +/-3.4% for Earth).

parameters.name: Areocentric solar longitude of perihelion
parameters.source: Allison & McEwen (2000)
parameters.notes: Near southern-summer solstice (Ls 270): southern summer is hot/short and hosts the dust-storm season. True anomaly nu = Ls - 251 deg.

parameters.name: Mean top-of-atmosphere solar flux at Mars
parameters.source: Appelbaum & Flood (1990), NASA TM-102299 (590/493/718 at TSI 1371) rescaled to TSI 1361: 586.2 mean, 490 aphelion, 713 perihelion
parameters.notes: Range = aphelion..perihelion. S_TOA(Ls) = 586.2*[(1+e*cos(Ls-251deg))/(1-e^2)]^2. Older sources using TSI 1371 give 590/497/723. verified correction: range endpoints made self-consistent with the stored formula (e=0.0934, S_mean 586.2): perihelion 586.2/(1-e)^2=713.2, aphelion 586.2/(1+e)^2=490.4 (was 493/717, which are TSI-1371 A&F endpoints). Note 'older sources 590/497/723' misquotes A&F (actual 590/493/718).

parameters.name: Surface pressure, low-elevation site (Gale, -4.5 km), annual mean
parameters.source: MSL REMS: Harri et al. (2014) JGR Planets; Haberle et al. (2014); Martinez et al. (2017) SSR 212
parameters.notes: Range = annual CO2 condensation cycle: min ~700-740 Pa at Ls~150, max ~925-955 Pa at Ls~250-260, secondary max ~890 Pa at Ls~55. ~25% of atmosphere cycles through polar caps. VL1 (-3.63 km): ~680-900 Pa. Scale to site elevation z with exp(-dz/11.1 km); datum reference 610.5 Pa. Diurnal tides add +/-2-5% (up to ~10% p-p at Gale).

parameters.name: Atmospheric pressure scale height
parameters.source: NSSDC Mars Fact Sheet
parameters.notes: Temperature dependent. Use for site-elevation pressure scaling. Surface air density rho = p*0.04334/(8.314*T) ~ 0.020 kg/m^3 at Gale (0.014-0.025 over diurnal/annual cycle) = ~1.6% of Earth sea level.

parameters.name: Atmospheric CO2 volume mixing ratio
parameters.source: MSL SAM QMS annual mean: Franz et al. (2017), Planet. Space Sci. 149, 169-175; Trainer et al. (2019) JGR Planets 124; original Mahaffy et al. (2013) Science 341
parameters.notes: Condensable; seasonal variation as CO2 cycles to polar caps (Trainer et al. 2019). Mean molar mass of atmosphere 43.34 g/mol. verified correction: prior source_url was Franz et al. 2015 (CO2=0.957); the stored 0.951 comes from the Franz et al. 2017 annual-mean paper (PII S0032063317300545). Value unchanged.

parameters.name: Atmospheric N2 volume mixing ratio
parameters.source: MSL SAM reevaluated annual mean: Franz et al. (2017), Planet. Space Sci. 149, 169-175; Trainer et al. (2019) JGR Planets 124 (Mahaffy 2013 gave 0.0189; Viking gave 0.027)
parameters.notes: Non-condensable: enriched ~+/-10-20% seasonally opposite the CO2 cycle. Feedstock for ISRU buffer gas (N2+Ar ~ 4.5% of intake). verified correction: prior source_url (PII S0032063315000495) is Franz et al. 2015, which reports N2=0.0203 - contradicting the stored 0.0259 by ~28%. Correct paper is Franz et al. 2017 (PII S0032063317300545), whose 2.59% N2 matches the stored value. Value unchanged.

parameters.name: Atmospheric Ar-40 volume mixing ratio
parameters.source: MSL SAM: Mahaffy et al. (2013) Science 341; confirmed by Franz et al. (2017)
parameters.notes: Inert seasonal tracer (Trainer et al. 2019). O2 = 0.16%, CO = 0.058% (minor species, not separate parameters).

parameters.name: Air temperature, typical daily max, southern summer (Gale-like mid-latitude, 1.6 m)
parameters.source: MSL REMS: Gomez-Elvira et al. (2014); Martinez et al. (2017) SSR
parameters.notes: Record highs ~+6 to +10 C at 2 pm LTST near perihelion. Winter (aphelion) daily max only 240-250 K. Site-specific: Gale is 4.6 S, -4.5 km (warm site).

parameters.name: Air temperature, typical nightly min, winter (Gale-like mid-latitude, 1.6 m)
parameters.source: MSL REMS: Martinez et al. (2017); frost-event analyses
parameters.notes: Summer nights bottom at 195-205 K. Diurnal air range 50-80 K in all seasons - the dominant thermal-cycling load. 40-50 deg latitude winter nights reach 150-170 K.

parameters.name: Ground surface temperature, midday max, summer (mid-latitude)
parameters.source: MSL REMS GTS (150-300 K instrument range): Hamilton et al. 2013; Martinez et al. (2017); NASA TFAWS 2024 MarsWRF worst-case hot 289.7 K
parameters.notes: Ground exceeds air by 15-30 K at midday (thin atmosphere decouples them). Winter midday: 255-270 K.

parameters.name: Ground surface temperature, pre-dawn min, winter (mid-latitude)
parameters.source: MSL REMS GTS; NASA TFAWS 2024 MarsWRF worst-case cold 129.5-139 K at -50 deg lat
parameters.notes: H2O frost observed at Gale when ground <170 K with high RH; CO2 frost floor ~148 K at 700-900 Pa. Global design cold case: 130 K.

parameters.name: Effective IR sky sink temperature (radiator boundary)
parameters.source: NASA TFAWS 2024 (Popok, MSFC) MarsWRF-derived boundary conditions: ~160 K predawn / ~186 K midday at tau=1, equator; MARSTHERM: ~140 K predawn clear sky
parameters.notes: Rises toward air temperature (~210-230 K) during global dust storms - radiators degrade exactly when solar power is scarce. Night radiator at 290 K, eps 0.9 rejects ~330 W/m^2.

parameters.name: Background dust optical depth, clear (aphelion) season, Ls 0-135
parameters.source: Montabone et al. (2015) Icarus 251 multiannual climatology (converted from 9.3 um CDOD); Lemmon et al. (2015) MER 5-Mars-year record
parameters.notes: Highly repeatable year-to-year. WARNING: orbital climatologies report IR absorption CDOD; visible extinction = ~2.6x that value. All sim values are visible extinction.

parameters.name: Background dust optical depth, dusty season, Ls 135-360 (no major storm)
parameters.source: Montabone et al. (2015); Lemmon et al. (2015): MER record spans 0.3-4.7 over 5 Mars years
parameters.notes: Punctuated by A (Ls~210-240), B (~250-270), C (~300-330) regional storm sequences every Mars year; regional storm peaks reach tau 2-5 for 15-40 sols.

parameters.name: Global dust storm peak optical depth
parameters.source: MY34/2018 event: tau ~8.5 measured at Gale (Guzewich et al. 2019 GRL; Viudez-Moreiras et al. 2019); >10.8 estimated at Meridiani (Opportunity, saturated)
parameters.notes: Design case: tau>=5 sustained >=60 sols with tau>=8 for ~30 sols. Diffuse light keeps ~15-25% of TOA insolation available even at peak. Storms cut daytime highs 10-20 K and raise night lows 5-10 K.

parameters.name: Probability of a planet-encircling dust storm in a given Mars year
parameters.notes: Aperiodic; onset confined to Ls ~185-310 (perihelion season). Model as Bernoulli/Poisson per Mars year within that Ls window.

parameters.name: Global dust storm duration (elevated tau above ~2x background)
